- For batter: 200g rice flour, 2 tbsp tapioca starch, ½ tsp salt, 500ml water  -  mix smooth
- 100g small dried shrimp, soaked, drained and pounded until fluffy
- 50g pork rinds (tóp mỡ), fried crispy
- Scallion oil (mỡ hành)
- Nước chấm for dipping
instructions:
1. Grease small saucers or ramekins.
2. Ladle a thin layer of batter into each saucer.
3. Steam over boiling water for 8–10 minutes until set and glossy on top.
4. Remove from steamer. Top each cake with dried shrimp floss, a piece of pork rind, and a spoonful of scallion oil.
5. Serve with nước chấm on the side for dipping.
tips: The batter layer should be thin  -  you want a delicate, slightly translucent cake. Too thick and it becomes chewy rather than silky.

name: Bánh Khọt
description: Crispy mini rice pancakes made in a special cast-iron mold, filled with shrimp and coconut milk batter  -  crunchy on the outside, custardy in the center. A Southern specialty from Vũng Tàu, eaten wrapped in lettuce with herbs and nước chấm.
tagline: Mini crispy pancakes from Vũng Tàu's street corners
ingredients:
- For batter: 200g rice flour, 1 tbsp tapioca starch, 200ml coconut milk, 200ml water, ½ tsp turmeric, ½ tsp salt
- 200g small shrimp, peeled
- 3 scallions, sliced
- Coconut oil or neutral oil for the mold
- To serve: lettuce, mint, perilla, nước chấm
instructions:
1. Whisk batter ingredients until smooth. Rest 20 minutes.
2. Heat bánh khọt mold (or takoyaki pan) over medium heat. Brush cavities generously with oil.
3. Fill each cavity halfway with batter. Place 1–2 shrimp in each. Top with a little scallion.
4. Cover and cook 3–4 minutes until edges are set and bottom is crispy.
5. Loosen with a toothpick and flip to crisp the other side briefly, 1 minute.
6. Serve hot with fresh herbs and nước chấm.
tips: The mold must be very hot and well-oiled before adding batter  -  this is what creates the crispy shell rather than a stuck, soggy pancake.

name: Bánh Bột Lọc
description: Translucent shrimp and pork dumplings from Huế  -  made with tapioca starch dough that becomes completely see-through when cooked, revealing the filling inside. Eaten with nước chấm, one at a time, in a state of near reverence.
tagline: Tapioca dumplings, translucent and chewy
ingredients:
- For dough: 200g tapioca starch + 50ml boiling water, mixed until a smooth, non-sticky dough forms (add water slowly)
- For filling: 150g shrimp, peeled and roughly chopped + 100g pork belly, finely diced + 1 tbsp fish sauce + 1 tsp sugar + white pepper
- Banana leaf or parchment paper for wrapping
- Nước chấm for serving
instructions:
1. Cook filling: stir-fry pork belly until rendered, add shrimp and season with fish sauce, sugar, pepper. Cool.
2. Divide dough into small pieces. Flatten each into a thin round on a greased surface.
3. Place 1 tsp filling in center. Fold over and seal edges tightly, pressing firmly.
4. Boil a large pot of water. Cook dumplings for 7–8 minutes until translucent and floating.
5. Remove with a slotted spoon. Serve with nước chấm.
tips: The dough dries out quickly  -  work with small portions and cover unused dough with plastic wrap. The water must be boiling hard or the dumplings will fall apart.

name: Gỏi Ngó Sen
description: Lotus stem salad with shrimp, pork, fresh herbs, and a bright lime-fish sauce dressing. The lotus stems are crunchy and slightly hollow  -  they hold the dressing and crunch against the tender shrimp and pork in a way that is deeply satisfying.
tagline: Lotus stem salad, light and elegant
ingredients:
- 300g fresh lotus stems, peeled and sliced diagonally (or canned, rinsed)
- 200g shrimp, peeled and cooked
- 150g pork belly, boiled and thinly sliced
- Small handful Vietnamese coriander (rau răm)
- Fried shallots, crushed peanuts
- For dressing: 3 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 garlic clove minced, 1 chili sliced
instructions:
1. If using fresh lotus stems: soak in water with a squeeze of lime for 10 minutes to prevent browning. Drain.
2. Blanch lotus stems in boiling water for 1–2 minutes until just tender-crisp. Drain and cool.
3. Mix dressing ingredients.
4. Toss lotus stems, shrimp, pork, and rau răm with dressing.
5. Taste and adjust.
6. Plate and top with fried shallots and peanuts.
tips: Don't overcook the lotus stems  -  the crunch is the entire point. 1–2 minutes maximum in boiling water.

name: Gỏi Bưởi Tôm Thịt
description: Pomelo salad with shrimp and pork  -  the citrus segments are separated into fluffy, slightly bitter individual sacs that hold dressing without going soggy. Combined with fresh herbs and a sweet-sour fish sauce dressing, it is one of the most refreshing Vietnamese salads.
tagline: Pomelo salad with shrimp  -  refreshing and bright
ingredients:
- 1 pomelo, peeled, segments broken into individual sacs by hand
- 200g shrimp, cooked and peeled
- 150g pork belly, boiled and sliced
- Fresh mint and Vietnamese coriander
- Fried shallots, crushed peanuts
- For dressing: 2 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 chili sliced
instructions:
1. Break pomelo segments apart into fluffy individual sacs. Remove any seeds or tough membrane.
2. Make dressing, adjusting sugar and lime to balance the pomelo's natural bitterness.
3. Toss pomelo, shrimp, pork, and fresh herbs gently in dressing.
4. Top with fried shallots and peanuts.
tips: The pomelo sacs should be feathery and separate, not crushed. Use your fingers to pull apart, not a knife  -  breaking them releases too much juice.

name: Phá Lấu
description: A Sài Gòn street snack of braised offal  -  pig ears, intestines, tofu, and quail eggs simmered in a spiced coconut-based broth with five spice and star anise. Sold from carts in little plastic bags with extra broth. More approachable than it sounds.
tagline: Offal braised in spiced coconut water  -  a Sài Gòn late-night classic
ingredients:
- 300g pig ears, cleaned and cut into strips
- 200g pig intestines, cleaned and cut into pieces
- 8 quail eggs, boiled and peeled
- 200g fried tofu puffs
- 200ml coconut water
- 3 tbsp soy sauce, 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar
- 3 star anise, 1 cinnamon stick, 3 cloves
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- 3 shallots, sliced
- Chili sauce and bánh mì for serving
instructions:
1. Blanch pig ears and intestines in boiling water for 10 minutes. Drain.
2. Heat oil in a pot. Fry shallots until golden. Add spices, fry 30 seconds.
3. Add all offal, coconut water, soy sauce, fish sauce, and sugar. Bring to a boil.
4. Reduce to low simmer. Add quail eggs and tofu puffs. Cook 45–50 minutes until tender.
5. Taste and adjust seasoning.
6. Serve in small bowls with extra braising liquid, chili sauce, and torn bánh mì.
tips: Cleaning the offal properly (blanching, scrubbing) is the only difficult part. Once that's done, the braise is forgiving.

name: Chè Bắp
description: Sweet corn dessert soup  -  fresh corn simmered with coconut milk and pandan until the kernels are tender and the liquid turns milky and fragrant. Simple, seasonal, and restorative. Eaten warm in the evening or cool in the afternoon.
tagline: Sweet corn pudding  -  summer in a bowl
ingredients:
- 3 ears fresh sweet corn, kernels cut off cobs (keep cobs)
- 400ml coconut milk
- 400ml water
- 3 tbsp sugar, or to taste
- 2 pandan leaves, tied in a knot
- Pinch of salt
- Salted coconut cream for topping (as in chè đậu đỏ)
instructions:
1. Simmer corn cobs in water with pandan leaves for 15 minutes to extract a sweet corn stock. Remove cobs.
2. Add corn kernels to the cob stock. Cook 8–10 minutes until tender.
3. Add coconut milk, sugar, and salt. Simmer 5 more minutes.
4. Taste  -  adjust sweetness. The soup should be gently sweet and fragrant.
5. Serve warm or at room temperature, topped with a spoonful of salted coconut cream.
tips: Simmering the cobs first pulls corn flavor into the liquid and makes a dramatically more flavorful base than starting with water alone.

name: Chè Trôi Nước
description: Glutinous rice dumplings filled with sweetened mung bean paste, floating in a warm ginger syrup with coconut cream drizzled on top. The dumplings bob and gleam  -  trôi nước means 'floating in water.' A Southern Vietnamese dessert that is also eaten at the winter solstice festival.
tagline: Floating rice dumplings in ginger syrup
ingredients:
- For dumplings: 200g glutinous rice flour + 120–130ml warm water  -  knead to a smooth dough
- For filling: 100g split mung beans, cooked until soft, mashed with 2 tbsp sugar and a drop of vanilla
- For ginger syrup: 600ml water + 80g sugar + 5cm fresh ginger, sliced and bruised
- Salted coconut cream, sesame seeds to finish
instructions:
1. Simmer ginger syrup ingredients for 15 minutes. Strain out ginger. Keep warm.
2. Portion mung bean filling into small balls, about 1 tsp each. Freeze briefly to firm.
3. Take a small piece of dough, flatten, wrap around a mung bean ball, seal and roll into a smooth sphere.
4. Boil a large pot of water. Cook dumplings until they float, then 2 more minutes. Remove.
5. Place in bowls of warm ginger syrup.
6. Drizzle salted coconut cream and scatter sesame seeds over.
tips: The dough should be soft and pliable but not sticky. Too dry and it cracks; too wet and it won't seal. Test one dumpling first.

name: Chè Đậu Xanh
description: Split mung bean sweet soup  -  the milder, softer sibling of chè đậu đỏ. The mung beans cook into a creamy, almost velvety texture when split, and the pandan and coconut cream make it fragrant and rich. One of Vietnam's most everyday desserts.
tagline: Mung bean sweet soup, cool and comforting
ingredients:
- 250g split mung beans (đậu xanh đã bỏ vỏ), soaked 1 hour
- 800ml water
- 60g sugar, or to taste
- 2 pandan leaves, tied
- Pinch of salt
- Salted coconut cream for topping
instructions:
1. Drain soaked mung beans. Add to pot with water and pandan leaves.
2. Bring to boil, simmer 25–30 minutes until completely soft and some beans have broken down into a creamy broth.
3. Add sugar and salt. Simmer 5 more minutes.
4. Remove pandan. Serve warm or at room temperature with salted coconut cream drizzled over.
tips: Split mung beans cook much faster than whole. Stir occasionally in the last 10 minutes as the thick beans are prone to catching on the bottom.

name: Bánh Da Lợn
description: Steamed layered rice cake with alternating translucent tapioca layers and opaque pandan or mung bean layers. Each layer is set before the next is poured  -  the result is a striped, jewel-like cake with a satisfyingly bouncy, jelly-like texture.
tagline: Layered taro cake, sliced and steamed
ingredients:
- For tapioca layer: 100g tapioca starch + 150ml coconut milk + 100ml water + 80g sugar + pinch of salt
- For pandan layer: 100g tapioca starch + 50g rice flour + 150ml coconut milk + 100ml pandan juice (blend 8 pandan leaves with water, strain) + 60g sugar
- For mung bean layer (optional): 150g cooked mashed mung beans mixed into second batter
instructions:
1. Mix each batter separately until very smooth. Strain.
2. Grease a round 20cm cake pan. Place in a steamer.
3. Pour a thin layer (about 80ml) of the first batter into the pan. Steam 5–6 minutes until just set.
4. Pour an equal layer of the second batter on top. Steam 5–6 minutes.
5. Alternate layers until all batter is used, steaming each layer fully before adding the next.
6. Let cool completely before cutting. Slice with an oiled knife.
tips: Each layer must be fully set before pouring the next or they will bleed into each other and lose the distinct stripes. Be patient between layers.

name: Bánh Trôi
description: Smooth glutinous rice balls filled with raw sugar (đường phên) or sweetened mung bean, boiled until they float and served in a pandan-scented syrup or plain. The Tết Hàn Thực dish  -  made on the Cold Food Festival in the third lunar month.
tagline: Sesame rice balls in sweet ginger broth
ingredients:
- 200g glutinous rice flour
- 120ml warm water
- 80g raw brown sugar (đường phên) or sweetened mung bean paste, portioned into small cubes
- Sesame seeds, toasted
- Shredded coconut (optional)
instructions:
1. Knead glutinous rice flour with warm water until smooth and pliable.
2. Divide into walnut-sized pieces. Flatten each, place a cube of sugar or bean paste in the center, seal tightly and roll smooth.
3. Boil in a large pot of water until balls float and skins are translucent, about 5–7 minutes.
4. Remove with a slotted spoon. Roll in toasted sesame seeds and coconut if using.
5. Serve as is, or in a light pandan syrup.
tips: Seal the filling completely  -  any gap will cause the sugar to leak into the water during cooking and you'll lose the sweet center.

name: Chè Thái
description: A Vietnamese-Thai inspired layered dessert in a glass  -  coconut milk over shaved ice with jackfruit, lychee, grass jelly, tapioca pearls, and palm seeds. Extravagant in ingredients, easy to assemble, and deeply refreshing in the heat.
tagline: Thai-style chè with coconut milk and jackfruit
ingredients:
- 200ml coconut milk + 2 tbsp sugar + pinch of salt, warmed and stirred
- 100g tapioca pearls, cooked until translucent, drained
- 100g grass jelly (sương sáo), cubed
- 100g canned jackfruit, sliced
- 100g canned lychee or longan, drained
- 50g palm seeds (hạt lựu) or water chestnuts
- Shaved or crushed ice
instructions:
1. Cook tapioca pearls per package until fully translucent. Drain and toss with a little sugar syrup to prevent sticking.
2. Prepare all toppings.
3. In each glass: layer grass jelly, jackfruit, lychee, tapioca pearls, and palm seeds.
4. Pack shaved ice over the top.
5. Pour sweetened coconut milk over everything.
6. Serve immediately with a long spoon.
tips: Prepare and chill all components before assembling. Everything should be cold when it meets the ice or it will melt too quickly.

name: Kem Dừa
description: Vietnamese coconut ice cream  -  dense, creamy, and aggressively coconut-forward, served inside a young coconut shell with toasted peanuts, coconut flesh strips, and rainbow jelly. The coconut shell is not decoration; eating the soft shell with the ice cream is the move.
tagline: Coconut ice cream in a coconut shell
ingredients:
- 400ml full-fat coconut milk
- 100ml coconut cream
- 80g sugar
- 2 tbsp coconut oil
- Pinch of salt
- 1 tsp vanilla
- To serve: toasted peanuts, strips of young coconut flesh, colored tapioca jelly
instructions:
1. Warm coconut milk, coconut cream, sugar, coconut oil, and salt in a saucepan until sugar dissolves. Don't boil.
2. Cool completely, then add vanilla.
3. Churn in an ice cream machine according to manufacturer instructions, or pour into a freezer container and stir every 30 minutes for 3 hours.
4. Serve scooped into a young coconut shell or bowl.
5. Top with peanuts, coconut flesh strips, and jelly.
tips: No-churn version: after cooling, whip the mixture lightly before freezing. Stir every 30 minutes for the first 3 hours to break up ice crystals.

name: Xôi Lá Dứa
description: Sticky rice colored and perfumed with fresh pandan juice  -  vibrant green, fragrant, and slightly sweet. Served with coconut cream and toasted sesame or mung bean crumble. Both dessert and breakfast, eaten from a small banana leaf.
tagline: Green rice scented with pandan
ingredients:
- 400g glutinous rice, soaked overnight
- Pandan juice: blend 8–10 pandan leaves with 200ml water, strain  -  deep green liquid
- 1 tsp salt
- Salted coconut cream topping
- Toasted sesame seeds or mung bean crumble
instructions:
1. Drain soaked rice. Toss with pandan juice (use enough to coat well and give a deep green color) and salt.
2. Steam over boiling water for 35–40 minutes, fluffing halfway, until cooked through and fragrant.
3. The rice should be uniformly green and sticky.
4. Serve warm, drizzled with salted coconut cream and scattered with sesame seeds.
tips: Fresh pandan juice gives a far more vibrant green and fragrant result than extract. Blend, strain, and use immediately  -  it loses color quickly.

name: Xôi Gấc
description: Red sticky rice made with gấc fruit  -  a spiky Southeast Asian fruit whose deep red-orange flesh produces a stunning crimson color in the rice, along with a subtle sweetness. Made for Tết and wedding celebrations, it is the most festive dish in Vietnamese cooking.
tagline: Red sticky rice for Tết and every celebration
ingredients:
- 400g glutinous rice, soaked overnight
- 3 tbsp gấc fruit flesh (from frozen gấc, or substitute 2 tbsp annatto oil for color, though flavor differs)
- 2 tbsp sugar
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- 1 tsp salt
- Coconut milk (optional)
instructions:
1. Drain soaked rice. Mix with gấc flesh, sugar, oil, and salt until the rice is evenly coated and deep red.
2. Steam over boiling water for 35–40 minutes, fluffing halfway through.
3. The rice should be a uniform deep crimson and completely sticky.
4. Serve in a mound, often shaped in a round bowl and turned out. Drizzle with coconut milk if desired.
tips: Wear gloves when handling gấc  -  the natural dye stains everything permanently. The color fades during cooking so start with more gấc than you think you need.

name: Bánh Bao
description: Vietnamese steamed buns  -  pillowy, slightly sweet white dough filled with a pork and egg filling. Sold from glass cases at every Vietnamese bakery and street corner. The soft-as-cloud exterior and dense, savory filling is a perfect portable meal.
tagline: Fluffy steamed bun, stuffed with pork and egg
ingredients:
- For dough: 300g all-purpose flour, 1 tsp instant yeast, 1 tbsp sugar, ½ tsp salt, ½ tsp baking powder, 160ml warm milk, 1 tbsp neutral oil
- For filling: 300g ground pork, 1 hard-boiled egg (quartered), 30g glass noodles (soaked, chopped), 20g wood ear mushroom (soaked, minced), 2 tbsp oyster sauce, 1 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tsp sugar, white pepper
instructions:
1. Mix dough ingredients, knead 8–10 minutes until smooth. Rest 1 hour until doubled.
2. Mix filling ingredients well. Divide into 8 portions. Wrap a piece of egg in each portion.
3. Punch down dough, divide into 8 pieces. Roll each into a round about 12cm.
4. Place filling in center, gather edges, pinch and twist to seal tightly.
5. Place on squares of parchment paper. Rest 15 minutes.
6. Steam over high heat for 15–18 minutes until puffed and cooked through.
tips: The dough should be slightly sweet and soft. If the bun deflates after steaming, the filling was too wet or the bun was opened too soon. Let rest 2 minutes before opening the steamer.

name: Nem Chua
description: Fermented pork roll  -  cured, slightly sour, pleasantly funky slices of ground pork mixed with shredded pork skin and garlic, wrapped in banana leaves and left to ferment for 3–5 days. A Southern snack eaten raw with fresh chili and garlic.
tagline: Fermented pork roll  -  sour, funky, beloved
ingredients:
- 500g pork loin, ground fine
- 150g pork skin, boiled until tender and shredded into thin strips
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- 1 tsp white pepper
- 1 tsp saltpeter (muối diêm, optional but traditional)
- 5 cloves garlic, thinly sliced
- Fresh chili slices
- Banana leaves for wrapping
instructions:
1. Mix ground pork with pork skin, fish sauce, sugar, pepper, and saltpeter until very sticky.
2. Portion into small cylinders (about 50g each).
3. Lay garlic and chili slices on a banana leaf. Place pork on top, roll tightly. Wrap in another leaf layer and secure with string or rubber bands.
4. Leave at room temperature (25–30°C) for 3–5 days. Taste from day 3.
5. Ready when the surface feels slightly tacky and the flavor is pleasantly sour and tangy.
6. Eat as is, with extra garlic and chili.
tips: Nem chua is eaten raw after fermentation  -  the acidity from the natural fermentation process cures the meat safely. In warm climates, 3 days is usually enough.

name: Bánh Giò
description: A pyramid-shaped dumpling from Northern Vietnam  -  tapioca and rice flour dough wrapped around a pork and mushroom filling, then folded in banana leaf and steamed until the exterior becomes smooth, slightly slippery, and entirely white. Unwrapped at the table.
tagline: Pyramid-shaped steamed cake, soft and savory
ingredients:
- For dough: 200g rice flour, 50g tapioca starch, 500ml water, 1 tbsp neutral oil, 1 tsp salt  -  cook over medium heat, stirring constantly until thick and translucent
- For filling: 200g ground pork, 30g wood ear mushrooms (soaked, minced), 2 shallots (minced), 1 tbsp fish sauce, white pepper
- Banana leaves, cut into 25cm squares, softened over flame
instructions:
1. Cook dough: combine flour, starch, water, oil, and salt in a pan. Stir constantly over medium heat until it becomes a thick, translucent paste that pulls away from the sides. Cool.
2. Cook filling: stir-fry shallots, add pork and mushrooms. Season with fish sauce and pepper. Cool.
3. Lay banana leaf. Spoon a portion of dough, flatten. Add filling in center. Fold dough around filling.
4. Fold banana leaf into a pyramid shape and secure.
5. Steam for 25–30 minutes. Serve in the leaf.
tips: The dough cooking step is the key technique  -  it must be cooked thoroughly or it will be gummy and fall apart when steamed.

name: Bạc Xỉu
description: A Sài Gòn original  -  more milk than coffee, sweetened with condensed milk and served over ice. Where cà phê sữa đá is coffee with milk, bạc xỉu is milk with coffee. The name is Cantonese-Vietnamese slang for 'a little dark.' Light, sweet, and perfect for those who find regular Vietnamese coffee too intense.
tagline: Milky coffee, sweeter and lighter than cà phê đen
ingredients:
- 1 shot strong Vietnamese coffee (from a phin, or espresso)
- 3–4 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- 100ml warm whole milk
- Ice
instructions:
1. Add condensed milk to a glass.
2. Brew coffee into a small amount and let cool slightly.
3. Fill glass with ice.
4. Pour warm milk over ice.
5. Add coffee last  -  pour slowly so it sits on top briefly before being stirred.
6. Stir and drink.
tips: The ratio is reversed from cà phê sữa đá  -  this should taste primarily of sweet milk with coffee as a note, not the other way around.

name: Sữa Đậu Nành
description: Homemade soy milk, warm and fragrant with pandan  -  a staple of Vietnamese morning street carts alongside bánh mì and xôi. Light, naturally sweet, and deeply comforting. Entirely different from store-bought soy milk.
tagline: Fresh soy milk, warm or cold
ingredients:
- 200g dried soybeans, soaked overnight in plenty of water
- 1.2L water
- 2 pandan leaves, tied
- 2–3 tbsp sugar, to taste
- Pinch of salt
instructions:
1. Drain and rinse soaked soybeans. Blend with 600ml fresh water in batches until very smooth.
2. Strain through a muslin cloth or fine mesh, squeezing to extract all milk. Discard pulp.
3. Pour soy milk into a pot with remaining water, pandan leaves, sugar, and salt.
4. Bring to a boil over medium heat, stirring, then reduce to a simmer for 10 minutes.
5. Remove pandan. Taste and adjust sweetness.
6. Serve warm or over ice.
tips: Must cook the raw soy milk thoroughly  -  raw soy contains compounds that can cause digestive upset. A full 10-minute simmer after it comes to a boil is essential.

name: Nước Mía
description: Fresh sugarcane juice pressed to order through a hand-cranked or electric press, served over ice with a squeeze of kumquat  -  one of the most refreshing drinks in Vietnam. The juice is grassy, sweet, faintly earthy, and nothing like any juice from a bottle.
tagline: Fresh sugarcane juice, crushed at the cart
ingredients:
- 2–3 stalks fresh sugarcane, peeled and cut into 15cm lengths
- 2–3 small kumquats or ½ lime
- Ice
instructions:
1. Feed sugarcane through a sugarcane juicer or heavy-duty juicer, pressing multiple times for maximum yield.
2. Pour juice over a glass of ice.
3. Squeeze a kumquat or lime over the top.
4. Stir and drink immediately  -  the juice oxidizes quickly.
tips: Drink within minutes of pressing  -  sugarcane juice oxidizes and loses its brightness rapidly. This is why it's always made fresh and consumed immediately.

name: Rau Má
description: Pennywort juice  -  blended fresh pennywort leaves with honey and a little lime, strained to a thin, vibrant green drink. Cooling and slightly bitter, consumed in Vietnamese food culture for its perceived health benefits. Drunk cold in the summer heat.
tagline: Pennywort juice  -  grassy, cool, good for you
ingredients:
- 100g fresh pennywort leaves (rau má), washed
- 400ml cold water
- 1 tbsp honey or sugar, to taste
- Juice of ½ lime
- Ice
instructions:
1. Blend pennywort leaves with cold water until very smooth.
2. Strain through a fine mesh to remove fibrous bits.
3. Add honey and lime juice. Stir well.
4. Taste and adjust sweetness.
5. Serve over ice immediately.
tips: Drink fresh  -  pennywort juice turns brown and bitter if left too long. Blend and serve within 15 minutes.

name: Trà Đào
description: Peach iced tea  -  Vietnamese-style with house-made peach syrup, cold-brewed jasmine tea, and a heap of ice. The style popularized by milk tea shops across Vietnam: sweet, fruity, and deeply refreshing. Simple to make at home and better than any chain version.
tagline: Peach iced tea  -  sweet, floral, everywhere in Hà Nội
ingredients:
- For peach syrup: 2 ripe peaches (or 1 can canned peaches) + 80g sugar + 100ml water  -  simmer 10 minutes, blend, strain
- 400ml cold-brewed jasmine tea (steep 2 tbsp jasmine tea in 400ml cold water for 8 hours)
- Ice
- Fresh peach slices and mint to serve
instructions:
1. Make peach syrup: simmer peaches, sugar, and water until fruit is soft. Blend smooth and strain. Cool.
2. Cold-brew jasmine tea overnight in the fridge.
3. Fill glasses with ice. Add 3–4 tbsp peach syrup per glass.
4. Pour cold jasmine tea over.
5. Stir, garnish with peach slices and mint.
tips: Cold-brewed tea is less bitter and more delicate than hot-brewed and cooled. The overnight steep is worth planning for.

name: Nước Sâm
description: A cooling herbal drink brewed from a combination of dried herbs  -  including imperata root, coix seeds, and dried sugarcane  -  believed in Vietnamese tradition to reduce body heat. Lightly sweet, slightly medicinal, and completely refreshing over ice.
tagline: Herbal drink, slow-simmered, served cold in summer
ingredients:
- 30g dried mía lau (dried sugarcane pieces)
- 20g bạch mao căn (imperata root)
- 20g ý dĩ (coix/job's tears)
- 1.2L water
- Rock sugar to taste
- 2 pandan leaves (optional)
instructions:
1. Rinse all dried herbs under cold water.
2. Combine herbs and water in a pot. Bring to a boil.
3. Reduce heat and simmer for 25–30 minutes.
4. Add rock sugar and pandan leaves if using. Simmer 5 more minutes.
5. Strain into a jug. Cool completely.
6. Serve over ice.
tips: Nước sâm mix is sold pre-packaged at Vietnamese grocery stores  -  look for herbal cooling tea blends if you can't source individual ingredients.

name: Sinh Tố Dâu
description: Vietnamese strawberry smoothie  -  blended with condensed milk, whole milk, and ice until thick and bright pink. Richer than a Western smoothie, sweeter than a milkshake, and sold from every trái cây sinh tố cart in Vietnam.
tagline: Strawberry smoothie, bright and sweet
ingredients:
- 150g fresh strawberries, hulled
- 2 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- 80ml whole milk
- 8–10 ice cubes
instructions:
1. Add all ingredients to a blender.
2. Blend on high until completely smooth and thick.
3. Taste  -  add more condensed milk if needed.
4. Pour into a glass and serve immediately.
tips: Vietnamese sinh tố should be thick enough to barely pour  -  if it's thin and watery, add more fruit or less milk. The condensed milk is the sweetener; no extra sugar needed.

name: Sinh Tố Xoài
description: Mango smoothie blended Vietnamese-style  -  with condensed milk and ice until thick and golden. Uses ripe, fragrant Vietnamese mango varieties like cát Hòa Lộc or Thái mango. Eaten as much as drunk.
tagline: Mango smoothie  -  tropical and thick
ingredients:
- 1 large ripe mango, peeled and diced (about 250g)
- 2 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- 60ml whole milk
- 8 ice cubes
instructions:
1. Blend mango, condensed milk, milk, and ice until thick and smooth.
2. Taste  -  ripe mango may need less condensed milk.
3. Pour into a glass. Serve immediately.
tips: The mango must be ripe to the point of being soft and deeply fragrant. Underripe mango makes a bland, stringy smoothie no amount of condensed milk can fix.

name: Trà Gừng Mật Ong
description: Ginger and honey tea  -  strong fresh ginger sliced and steeped in hot water with honey and a squeeze of lime. A winter warming drink in Northern Vietnam, drunk when cold or under the weather. Aggressively gingery, sweet, and clarifying.
tagline: Ginger honey tea for cold mornings
ingredients:
- 5cm fresh ginger, peeled and thinly sliced
- 400ml water
- 2 tbsp honey, or to taste
- Juice of ½ lime
- Optional: 1 lemongrass stalk, bruised
instructions:
1. Bring water to a boil with ginger (and lemongrass if using). Simmer 8–10 minutes.
2. Remove from heat. Add honey and lime juice.
3. Stir until honey dissolves.
4. Strain into cups.
5. Drink hot.
tips: Add honey off the heat to preserve its flavor compounds. Boiling honey dulls it. The tea should be assertively spicy from the ginger  -  adjust quantity to taste.

name: Cà Phê Dừa
description: Coconut coffee  -  a Hội An specialty that has swept Vietnam. Strong cold-brew or iced Vietnamese coffee blended with coconut cream and ice until thick and frothy, poured over more ice. Somewhere between a coffee and a coconut smoothie.
tagline: Iced coffee blended with coconut milk
ingredients:
- 1 shot strong Vietnamese coffee, cooled
- 100ml coconut cream
- 1 tbsp sweetened condensed milk (or coconut sugar)
- 8 ice cubes
- Extra ice for serving
instructions:
1. Brew coffee and cool to room temperature.
2. Blend cooled coffee, coconut cream, condensed milk, and ice until thick and frothy.
3. Pour over a glass of fresh ice.
4. Drink immediately.
tips: Use full-fat coconut cream, not coconut milk  -  the thickness is what gives this its distinctive creamy texture. Coconut milk will make it watery.

name: Trà Sữa Trân Châu
description: Vietnamese bubble tea  -  jasmine or oolong tea mixed with condensed milk and tapioca pearls. The Vietnamese version tends to be less sweet than Taiwanese, with a stronger tea flavor and the signature Vietnamese condensed milk richness.
tagline: Milk tea with tapioca pearls  -  a Việt Nam institution
ingredients:
- 100g black tapioca pearls
- 400ml strong-brewed jasmine or oolong tea, cooled
- 4 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- 100ml whole milk
- Ice
- Brown sugar syrup for pearls: 3 tbsp brown sugar + 2 tbsp water, simmered to a light syrup
instructions:
1. Cook tapioca pearls per package (usually 15–20 minutes until fully cooked through with no white center). Drain and toss in brown sugar syrup while warm.
2. Brew tea, cool completely.
3. Mix cooled tea with condensed milk and whole milk. Stir well.
4. Add ice to glasses. Spoon pearls in first.
5. Pour tea mixture over.
6. Serve with a fat straw.
tips: Tapioca pearls are best eaten within 2–4 hours of cooking  -  they harden in the fridge. Make fresh each time.

name: Hủ Tiếu Xào
description: Stir-fried rice noodles with pork, shrimp, bean sprouts, and chives  -  the drier, wok-fried version of hủ tiếu that's cooked over high heat until the noodles get some char at the edges. A quick, satisfying noodle dish that showcases the wok.
tagline: Stir-fried noodles, fast and satisfying
ingredients:
- 300g fresh rice noodles (hủ tiếu tươi) or flat rice noodles, softened
- 150g pork shoulder, thinly sliced
- 150g shrimp, peeled
- 100g bean sprouts
- Handful garlic chives or scallions, cut into 3cm lengths
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 tbsp oyster sauce
- 1 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tsp sesame oil
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
instructions:
1. Heat wok over highest heat until smoking. Add oil.
2. Stir-fry garlic 20 seconds. Add pork slices, cook until just done.
3. Add shrimp, cook 1 minute.
4. Add noodles. Toss and spread across wok surface  -  let sit 30–45 seconds without touching to get char.
5. Add oyster sauce, soy sauce. Toss vigorously.
6. Add bean sprouts and chives in the last minute. Toss once.
7. Finish with sesame oil. Serve immediately.
tips: Patience is key  -  let the noodles sit against the hot wok surface to char before tossing. Constant stirring prevents the char and kills the wok hei.

name: Cháo Lòng
description: Offal congee  -  silky pork rice porridge topped with sliced pork intestines, heart, liver, and blood pudding. A deeply savory, rich breakfast for people who know what they like. Eaten from carts in Sài Gòn alongside quẩy (Chinese fried dough) for dipping.
tagline: Offal congee  -  adventurous, warming, deeply satisfying
ingredients:
- 200g jasmine rice
- 1.5L pork stock
- 150g pork intestines, cleaned and cut into rings
- 100g pork heart, sliced
- 100g pork liver, thinly sliced
- 50g blood pudding (tiết canh set), sliced (optional)
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, salt, white pepper
- Ginger, 3cm, bruised
- Fried shallots, sliced scallions, fresh ginger julienned, cilantro, lime to serve
instructions:
1. Simmer rice in pork stock with ginger over medium-low heat for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally, until porridge is thick and smooth.
2. Blanch intestines and heart in boiling salted water for 5 minutes. Drain.
3. Season porridge with fish sauce, salt, and white pepper.
4. In individual bowls: ladle porridge, top with intestines, heart, and a few slices of raw liver (which cooks in the hot porridge).
5. Add blood pudding if using, fried shallots, scallions, and ginger.
6. Serve with quẩy, lime, and extra chili.
tips: Thorough cleaning of the intestines is essential  -  turn inside out, rub with salt and cornstarch, rinse repeatedly. The blanching step removes any residual odor.

name: Bánh Canh Chả Cá
description: Thick noodle soup with fish cakes  -  a Central Vietnamese specialty from Nha Trang and Quy Nhơn. Golden-fried fish cakes float in a clear, sweet fish stock alongside thick tapioca noodles, eaten with lime and chili.
tagline: Thick noodles with fish cake  -  a coastal staple
ingredients:
- For fish cakes: 400g firm white fish fillet (mackerel or Spanish mackerel), minced + 1 tbsp fish sauce + 1 tsp sugar + white pepper + 2 tbsp neutral oil  -  mix until sticky, form patties
- 500g fresh bánh canh noodles
- 1L fish or pork stock
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tsp sugar
- Lemongrass and shallots for stock aromatics
- Scallions, fried shallots, lime, chili
instructions:
1. Mix fish cake ingredients vigorously until sticky. Shape into patties or balls.
2. Pan-fry fish cakes in oil until golden on both sides. Set aside.
3. Simmer stock with lemongrass and shallots for 20 minutes. Strain and season.
4. Cook bánh canh noodles in the simmering broth for 5–7 minutes until translucent.
5. Divide noodles into bowls. Add fish cakes and ladle broth over.
6. Top with scallions and fried shallots. Serve with lime and chili.
tips: Mix the fish paste very well  -  the more you mix, the bouncier and more firm the fish cakes become. Undermixed fish cakes will be crumbly.

name: Lươn Xào Sả Ớt
description: Eel stir-fried with lemongrass and chili  -  a Northern Vietnamese specialty found in Hà Nội's eel noodle restaurants and home kitchens. The eel is meaty, slightly sweet, and takes lemongrass and chili exceptionally well. Served over rice or noodles.
tagline: Spicy eel stir-fry  -  bold, fiery, addictive
ingredients:
- 400g freshwater eel, cleaned and cut into 4cm pieces
- 3 stalks lemongrass, white parts minced
- 3 dried red chilies or 1 fresh chili, sliced
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- 1 tbsp turmeric powder
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- Fresh dill and scallions to finish
instructions:
1. Toss eel pieces with turmeric, a pinch of salt, and 1 tbsp fish sauce. Marinate 15 minutes.
2. Heat oil in a wok or pan over high heat. Add garlic and lemongrass, stir-fry until fragrant and golden.
3. Add eel, spreading in a single layer. Cook without moving for 2 minutes to form a crust.
4. Toss and continue cooking 5–6 minutes until cooked through and slightly caramelized.
5. Add remaining fish sauce, sugar, and chili. Toss to combine.
6. Finish with dill and scallions. Serve over rice or rice noodles.
tips: Fresh or live eel gives the best texture. Frozen eel works but releases more water during cooking  -  pat very dry before marinating.

name: Hàu Nướng Mỡ Hành
description: Grilled oysters with scallion oil and crushed peanuts  -  a coastal Vietnamese staple from Nha Trang and Phú Quốc. Live oysters grilled over charcoal until the edges curl and bubble, then doused in sizzling scallion oil and topped with peanuts.
tagline: Grilled oysters with scallion oil  -  seaside luxury
ingredients:
- 12 fresh oysters, on the half shell
- Scallion oil (mỡ hành)  -  4 scallion stalks sliced + 3 tbsp oil heated until smoking
- 3 tbsp crushed roasted peanuts
- 1 tbsp fried shallots
- Sriracha or chili sauce
- Lime wedges
instructions:
1. Prepare scallion oil: heat oil until smoking, pour over sliced scallions. It will sizzle. Set aside.
2. Place oysters on a grill over high charcoal heat (or in a very hot oven).
3. Grill until the oyster juices bubble and the edges just begin to curl, 4–6 minutes.
4. Remove carefully. Spoon scallion oil over each oyster immediately.
5. Top with crushed peanuts, fried shallots, and a few drops of sriracha.
6. Squeeze lime over. Eat in one move.
tips: Don't overcook  -  oysters should be just set, not rubbery. The moment the edges begin to curl is the moment to remove them.

name: Gỏi Sứa
description: Jellyfish salad  -  translucent strips of jellyfish dressed with sesame oil, chili, and fish sauce, tossed with pork, shrimp, herbs, and peanuts. The jellyfish is firm and crunchy in a way that no other ingredient can replicate. A beloved Southern snack that rewards the adventurous.
tagline: Jellyfish salad  -  chewy, cool, an acquired joy
ingredients:
- 200g prepared jellyfish strips (packed in salt, soaked and rinsed thoroughly)
- 150g cooked shrimp, peeled
- 100g pork belly, boiled and sliced
- 1 carrot, julienned
- Fresh Vietnamese coriander (rau răm) and mint
- 1 tbsp sesame oil
- For dressing: 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 garlic clove minced, 1 chili sliced
- Crushed peanuts, fried shallots
instructions:
1. Soak packaged jellyfish in cold water for 30 minutes, changing water twice to remove salt. Drain well.
2. Blanch jellyfish briefly in hot (not boiling) water for 30 seconds  -  this firms the texture. Drain and cool.
3. Toss with sesame oil.
4. Mix dressing ingredients.
5. Combine jellyfish, shrimp, pork, carrot, and herbs. Pour dressing over and toss.
6. Top with peanuts and fried shallots.
tips: Don't over-blanch the jellyfish  -  it should be firm and slightly crunchy, not soft. Hot water for 30 seconds maximum.

name: Bún Kèn
description: Phú Quốc's famous fish and coconut noodle soup  -  fish poached in a coconut milk broth fragrant with lemongrass, turmeric, and galangal, poured over rice vermicelli with a pile of fresh herbs and crunchy accompaniments. Little known outside the island.
tagline: Coconut milk broth, steamed fish, Phú Quốc's pride
ingredients:
- 400g firm white fish (Spanish mackerel or tuna), poached and flaked
- 400ml coconut milk
- 300ml fish or pork stock
- 2 stalks lemongrass, bruised
- 2cm galangal, sliced
- 1 tsp turmeric powder
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tsp sugar
- 400g rice vermicelli, cooked
- To serve: bean sprouts, shredded banana blossom, cucumber, fresh mint, lime, sliced chili
instructions:
1. Simmer lemongrass, galangal, and turmeric in stock for 15 minutes. Strain.
2. Add coconut milk to the strained stock. Bring to a gentle simmer.
3. Add flaked fish. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
4. Simmer 5 minutes  -  don't boil or the coconut milk will curdle.
5. Divide cooked bún into bowls. Ladle coconut broth and fish over.
6. Serve with all the accompaniments piled on the side.
tips: The broth should be gently simmered, never boiled, once the coconut milk is added. A rolling boil will break the emulsion and make it grainy.

name: Cơm Niêu
description: Clay pot rice  -  jasmine rice cooked in a small clay pot over charcoal until the bottom layer forms a thin, crispy golden crust (cơm cháy). The crust is the prize: scraped from the bottom and eaten with the rice, or soaked with broth and eaten as a separate crunchy snack.
tagline: Clay pot rice with a crispy bottom  -  worth the wait
ingredients:
- 300g jasmine rice, rinsed
- 350ml water
- 1 tsp neutral oil
- Pinch of salt
- Serve with: any Vietnamese braised dish, pickled vegetables, fish sauce
instructions:
1. Combine rice, water, oil, and salt in a small clay pot or heavy-bottomed pot.
2. Bring to a boil uncovered, then reduce to lowest heat. Cover tightly.
3. Cook 20 minutes on very low heat.
4. Increase heat to medium for the final 5 minutes  -  this crisps the bottom layer.
5. Turn off heat. Let rest 10 minutes with lid on.
6. Serve from the pot: fluffy rice first, then scrape up the golden crust.
tips: The crust forms from the direct heat contact. Too low and no crust forms; too high and it burns. The 5-minute medium-heat blast at the end is the technique.

name: Chè Sương Sáo
description: Grass jelly drink  -  black, slightly bitter grass jelly cubed and served in a tall glass of sweetened coconut milk over ice. Cooling, deeply refreshing, and one of the simplest Vietnamese dessert drinks. The grass jelly wobbles; the coconut milk is cold and sweet; the ice melts just slowly enough.
tagline: Grass jelly drink, dark and mysteriously refreshing
ingredients:
- 200g grass jelly (sương sáo), store-bought, cut into small cubes
- 300ml coconut milk
- 2 tbsp sugar or simple syrup, to taste
- Pinch of salt
- Ice
instructions:
1. Mix coconut milk with sugar and a pinch of salt. Stir until sugar dissolves. Chill.
2. Cut grass jelly into 1cm cubes.
3. Fill tall glasses with ice.
4. Add grass jelly cubes.
5. Pour sweetened coconut milk over.
6. Stir gently and drink immediately.
tips: The salted coconut milk makes the sweetness more complex and rounds out the slight bitterness of the grass jelly. Don't skip the salt.

name: Bún Thang
description: The most elegant noodle soup of Hà Nội  -  thin rice vermicelli in a clear, golden chicken-and-shrimp broth, topped with strips of omelette, shredded chicken, chả lụa, and fragrant dried shrimp. A dish of contrasts: delicate broth, complex toppings, finished with a dot of mắm tôm.
tagline: Hà Nội's most refined noodle soup
ingredients:
- 1 whole chicken
- 300g dried shrimp
- 1L chicken stock
- 300g thin rice vermicelli (bún)
- 3 eggs, beaten
- 150g chả lụa, thinly sliced
- 4 spring onions
- Fish sauce, salt, sugar to taste
- Mắm tôm (shrimp paste), chilli, lime to serve
instructions:
1. Simmer chicken in water with dried shrimp for 60 minutes. Skim constantly for a clear broth. Remove chicken, shred finely.
2. Make a thin omelette with the eggs; roll and slice into thin ribbons.
3. Strain broth; season with fish sauce, sugar, salt until lightly sweet and salty.
4. Cook vermicelli per packet, rinse cold.
5. Arrange noodles in bowls. Top with chicken, omelette ribbons, chả lụa slices, dried shrimp. Ladle hot broth over.
6. Serve with a tiny side of mắm tôm diluted with lime and chilli.
tips: The broth must be absolutely clear  -  skim religiously and never let it boil hard. A cloudy bún thang is a sad bún thang.

name: Phở Cuốn
description: A Hà Nội invention born in the 1990s: fresh wide phở sheets (not in broth) rolled around stir-fried beef, herbs, and bean sprouts, then dipped in a light nuoc cham. Street food that became a city obsession.
tagline: Fresh phở sheets rolled and dipped, Hà Nội style
ingredients:
- 400g fresh phở sheets (wide banh pho)
- 200g beef sirloin, sliced very thin
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tbsp oyster sauce
- 1 tsp soy sauce
- Bean sprouts, fresh mint, perilla leaves, coriander
- Nuoc cham for dipping
instructions:
1. Stir-fry garlic in oil until golden. Add beef slices, oyster sauce, soy  -  toss on high heat for 1–2 minutes.
2. Lay a phở sheet flat. Place herbs, a few bean sprouts, and a few beef slices at one edge.
3. Roll tightly. Cut or leave whole.
4. Serve immediately with nuoc cham for dipping.
tips: The phở sheets must be fresh and room temperature  -  they tear when cold. Eat within minutes of rolling.

name: Bún Ngan
description: A Hà Nội autumn specialty  -  muscovy duck simmered in a deeply savoury broth with fermented tofu and turmeric, served over rice vermicelli with a tangle of fresh herbs. Rich, funky, and warming.
tagline: Muscovy duck noodle soup with fermented tofu
ingredients:
- 1 muscovy duck (ngan), cut into pieces
- 2 blocks fermented tofu (chao)
- 1 tsp turmeric
- 2 lemongrass stalks, bruised
- 400g rice vermicelli
- Fresh dill, perilla, shredded banana blossom
- Fish sauce, shrimp paste to taste
instructions:
1. Brown duck pieces in oil. Add fermented tofu, mashing into the oil. Add turmeric and lemongrass.
2. Cover with water; simmer 50–60 minutes until duck is tender. Skim fat.
3. Season broth with fish sauce and a little shrimp paste.
4. Cook noodles per packet, divide into bowls.
5. Add duck pieces and ladle over broth. Serve with fresh herbs.
tips: Muscovy duck has very little fat compared to Pekin duck  -  the broth stays cleaner. The fermented tofu is non-negotiable.

name: Miến Lươn
description: Glass noodle soup with freshwater eel  -  a Hà Nội classic that fills up the city's alleyway restaurants on cool evenings. The eel is stir-fried with turmeric and served in a clear, sweet broth over silky mung bean noodles.
tagline: Glass noodles in clear broth with turmeric eel
ingredients:
- 400g freshwater eel, cleaned and cut into 5cm pieces
- 200g mung bean glass noodles
- 1 tsp turmeric
- 1 tsp fish sauce
- 1L clear pork or chicken stock
- Fresh dill, fried shallots, spring onion
- Chilli, pepper to serve
instructions:
1. Marinate eel with turmeric, fish sauce, and a pinch of salt for 15 minutes.
2. Fry eel in a little oil until golden and fragrant. Set aside.
3. Bring stock to a simmer; season with fish sauce and salt.
4. Soak glass noodles in hot water for 10 minutes; drain.
5. Serve noodles in bowls, top with eel pieces. Ladle hot stock over.
6. Finish with fresh dill, fried shallots, and fresh chilli.
tips: Fresh eel is best. If unavailable, smoked eel works beautifully  -  skip the turmeric fry and just warm it in the broth.

name: Bánh Đa Cua
description: The signature noodle of Hải Phòng  -  wide, chewy reddish-brown noodles in a brick-red crab and tomato broth, topped with sea crab, water spinach, and fried dough sticks. Bold, savoury, completely its own thing.
tagline: Hải Phòng crab noodle soup with thick brown noodles
ingredients:
- 400g dried bánh đa đỏ (red/brown flat noodles)
- 500g sea crab meat and roe
- 3 tomatoes, chopped
- 1 tbsp tomato paste
- 200g water spinach, blanched
- 2 tbsp fermented crab paste (mắm cua)
- 1L pork broth
- Fried dough sticks (quẩy), shallots, chilli to serve
instructions:
1. Fry tomatoes in oil until they collapse into a paste. Add tomato paste and fermented crab paste.
2. Add pork broth; bring to a simmer and cook 20 minutes.
3. Add crab meat and roe; simmer 10 more minutes. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
4. Cook thick noodles according to packet; divide into bowls.
5. Ladle broth over noodles. Top with water spinach, extra crab, fried shallots, and broken pieces of quẩy.
tips: Bánh đa đỏ noodles are thick and chewy  -  nothing else substitutes well. Fermented crab paste (mắm cua) gives the broth its irreplaceable depth.

name: Bún Đậu Mắm Tôm
description: A Hà Nội street staple that divides the room: cold rice vermicelli served with golden fried tofu, chả cốm, nem chua, and a bold, pungent shrimp paste (mắm tôm) dipping sauce. No heat, no broth  -  just textures, herbs, and that funky sauce.
tagline: Cold vermicelli with fried tofu and shrimp paste
ingredients:
- 300g rice vermicelli (bún)
- 400g firm tofu, cut in cubes
- 100g chả cốm (green rice sausage, optional)
- Mắm tôm (shrimp paste), lime juice, sugar, chilli for dipping sauce
- Mint, perilla, cucumber slices to serve
- Oil for frying
instructions:
1. Fry tofu in hot oil until golden on all sides. Drain on paper.
2. Make dipping sauce: whisk mắm tôm with lime juice, a pinch of sugar, and sliced chilli. It should be pungent and tangy.
3. Cook vermicelli per packet; rinse cold; arrange on a large plate.
4. Arrange fried tofu, sliced chả cốm, and fresh herbs alongside.
5. Dip everything into the mắm tôm sauce.
tips: The mắm tôm sauce is an acquired taste  -  but once you're a convert, no other dipping sauce will do. The lime juice cuts the pungency just enough.

name: Bún Ốc
description: A beloved Hà Nội street soup: tangy tomato broth with freshwater snails over rice vermicelli, topped with fried tofu puffs that soak up the sour broth. Eaten at tiny pavement stools on rainy afternoons.
tagline: Sour tomato snail noodle soup, a Hà Nội icon
ingredients:
- 500g freshwater snails (ốc), cleaned
- 4 tomatoes, cut into wedges
- 200g fried tofu puffs
- 300g rice vermicelli
- 1 tbsp vinegar or tamarind water
- Fish sauce, spring onion, chilli, fresh herbs to serve
- 1L light broth
instructions:
1. Stir-fry snails with garlic and fish sauce until fragrant. Set aside.
2. Fry tomatoes in oil until jammy. Add broth; simmer 20 minutes.
3. Add vinegar or tamarind; adjust acidity  -  the broth should be noticeably sour.
4. Add fried tofu puffs; simmer 5 minutes to absorb the broth.
5. Cook vermicelli; serve in bowls. Ladle broth over. Top with snails, tofu, spring onion, and fresh herbs.
tips: The sourness is the soul of this dish  -  don't hold back on the vinegar. Fried tofu puffs absorb the tangy broth like little sponges.

name: Mì Vằn Thắn
description: Northern Vietnam's take on wonton noodles  -  egg noodles in a clear pork broth with hand-folded dumplings stuffed with pork and wood ear mushroom, topped with slices of char siu and a soft-boiled egg. Inherited from Hà Nội's Chinese community.
tagline: Wonton noodle soup, Hà Nội Cantonese heritage
ingredients:
- 400g fresh egg noodles
- 300g ground pork
- 20 wonton wrappers
- 2 tbsp dried wood ear mushroom, soaked and minced
- 1L clear pork broth
- 200g char siu (red-braised pork), sliced
- 2 soft-boiled eggs, halved
- Spring onion, white pepper, soy sauce to serve
instructions:
1. Mix ground pork with minced wood ear mushroom, soy sauce, white pepper, and a pinch of sesame oil.
2. Place a teaspoon of filling in each wonton wrapper; fold and seal edges with water.
3. Boil wontons in salted water until they float, 4–5 minutes. Remove.
4. Cook egg noodles per packet.
5. Heat broth; season with soy and a little salt.
6. Serve noodles in bowls, add wontons, char siu slices, egg. Ladle hot broth over.
tips: Use the wonton cooking water as part of the broth  -  the starch from the wontons gives the soup a slightly silky texture.

name: Cháo Sườn
description: Pork rib congee, cooked slowly until the grains dissolve into silk, with the ribs braised tender and falling off the bone. Served in Hà Nội's street stalls from 5am, eaten before the city fully wakes.
tagline: Silky pork rib congee for early Hà Nội mornings
ingredients:
- 200g jasmine rice
- 500g pork spare ribs, cut into sections
- 1.8L water
- 3cm knob ginger, bruised
- Fish sauce, salt, white pepper
- Fried shallots, spring onion, coriander to serve
instructions:
1. Parboil ribs in boiling water for 5 minutes; discard water and rinse ribs clean.
2. Bring 1.8L fresh water with ginger to a boil; add ribs. Simmer on low for 30 minutes.
3. Add rinsed rice; cook a further 35–40 minutes, stirring occasionally, until porridge is thick and creamy.
4. Season with fish sauce, salt, white pepper.
5. Serve in deep bowls, topped with fried shallots, spring onion, and coriander.
tips: Parboiling the ribs first is essential  -  it removes impurities that would make the broth murky and bitter.

name: Xôi Đỗ Xanh
description: Sticky rice steamed with mung bean until both are perfectly tender, then pressed gently together and finished with fried shallots and a drizzle of oil. One of the most comforting breakfasts in northern Vietnam.
tagline: Mung bean sticky rice, northern breakfast staple
ingredients:
- 400g glutinous rice, soaked overnight
- 200g split mung beans, soaked 2 hours
- 1 tsp salt
- Fried shallots, neutral oil to finish
instructions:
1. Drain rice and mung beans separately. Season rice with salt.
2. Steam mung beans for 20 minutes until soft.
3. Mix mung beans into rice. Steam together for a further 30 minutes until rice is translucent and tender.
4. Gently press and fold so beans are evenly distributed but the texture isn't mashed.
5. Serve in bowls topped with fried shallots and a little oil.
tips: Don't over-stir  -  you want distinct grains of rice studded with creamy mung bean, not a uniform paste.

name: Xôi Ngũ Sắc
description: Five-color sticky rice dyed naturally with butterfly pea flowers (blue), turmeric (yellow), dragon fruit or magenta plant (red/purple), pandan (green), and left plain (white). A festival and ritual dish from the northern highlands, beautiful beyond its simplicity.
tagline: Five-colour festival sticky rice from the highlands
ingredients:
- 500g glutinous rice, soaked overnight, divided into 5 portions
- Butterfly pea flowers (dried), steeped → blue
- 1 tsp turmeric powder → yellow
- Magenta plant leaves or beet juice → red/purple
- 3 pandan leaves, blended with water → green
- Leave one portion plain → white
- Salt to taste
instructions:
1. Steep each natural dye in hot water; strain. Soak each rice portion in its dye colour for 30–60 minutes.
2. Drain each portion. Season with a pinch of salt.
3. Steam each portion separately (or together in partitioned sections) for 35–40 minutes.
4. Arrange the five colours together in concentric rings or sections on a platter.
tips: Natural dyes fade a little during steaming  -  make the soaked raw rice very vivid and the final colour will be just right.

name: Cháo Hàu
description: Oyster congee from the northern coast  -  plump fresh oysters stirred into silky rice porridge just before serving, finished with ginger oil and white pepper. A briny, warming bowl that tastes entirely of the sea.
tagline: Coastal oyster congee with ginger oil
ingredients:
- 180g jasmine rice
- 250g fresh oysters, shucked
- 1.5L light stock or water
- 2cm knob ginger, julienned
- 2 spring onions, sliced
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- Fish sauce, white pepper, salt
instructions:
1. Bring stock to a boil; add rinsed rice. Cook on medium-low, stirring occasionally, for 40 minutes until silky.
2. Heat oil in a small pan; fry ginger until crispy and golden. Set aside.
3. Season congee with fish sauce and salt.
4. Stir in oysters; cook just 1–2 minutes until they curl and are barely cooked through.
5. Serve immediately topped with ginger oil, crispy ginger, spring onion, and white pepper.
tips: Oysters overcook in seconds  -  add them last and serve immediately. A slightly underdone oyster in hot congee is perfect.

name: Bánh Gai
description: A dense, jet-black sticky rice cake made with ramie (gai) leaves that dye the dough dark, wrapped in banana leaves and steamed. Filled with sweetened mung bean, coconut, and sesame. A northern speciality from Bắc Ninh  -  chewy, earthy, and faintly bitter.
tagline: Black sticky rice cake wrapped in banana leaf
ingredients:
- 400g glutinous rice flour
- 100g dried ramie (gai) leaves, powdered, or use black sesame paste as substitute
- 200g split mung bean, steamed and mashed
- 50g desiccated coconut
- 3 tbsp sesame seeds, toasted
- 100g palm sugar or brown sugar
- Banana leaves for wrapping
instructions:
1. Combine mung bean paste with coconut, sesame, and sugar. Roll into 8 small balls.
2. Mix glutinous rice flour with gai leaf powder (or black sesame paste) and enough warm water to form a soft, pliable dough. Knead 10 minutes.
3. Divide dough into 8 portions; flatten each and wrap around a mung bean ball. Pinch to seal.
4. Wrap each cake in softened banana leaf into a square parcel.
5. Steam for 45–50 minutes. Rest 10 minutes before opening.
tips: The banana leaf imparts a faint grassy flavour  -  don't skip it. Softened leaves in boiling water become pliable and won't crack.

name: Bánh Khúc
description: A spring delicacy of northern Vietnam  -  sticky rice cakes stuffed with mung bean paste and pork fat, coated in a fine layer of cudweed (khúc) herb that turns them grey-green and fragrant. Sold from baskets in Hà Nội markets during the brief khúc season.
tagline: Cudweed herb sticky rice dumplings, a Hà Nội spring treat
ingredients:
- 300g glutinous rice flour
- 50g dried cudweed (khúc) herb, ground, or fresh if available
- 200g mung bean paste (steamed mung bean + sugar)
- 100g pork belly or lard, diced small
- 200g glutinous rice (outer coating)
- Salt to taste
instructions:
1. Combine glutinous rice flour with ground cudweed herb and enough warm water to make a soft dough.
2. Mix mung bean paste with a pinch of salt. Mix in diced pork belly.
3. Divide dough into 10 portions; flatten and fill with mung bean mixture. Pinch to seal into a round.
4. Roll each dumpling in raw glutinous rice until fully coated.
5. Steam for 40–45 minutes until rice coating is fully cooked and translucent.
tips: The glutinous rice coating is what makes these distinctive  -  press it firmly onto the dough so it adheres through steaming.

name: Bánh Đúc
description: A savoury rice cake that sets in a flat pan, sliced into wedges, and dressed with pork and mushroom stir-fry, peanuts, and a sweet-salty dressing. The texture is silky and dense  -  halfway between tofu and polenta.
tagline: Savoury rice cake with pork and peanut topping
ingredients:
- 200g rice flour
- 50g tapioca starch
- 800ml water
- ½ tsp salt
- 150g minced pork
- 5 dried shiitake mushrooms, soaked and diced
- Roasted peanuts, fried shallots, spring onion
- Nuoc cham dressing to serve
instructions:
1. Whisk rice flour, tapioca starch, salt, and water until smooth.
2. Cook in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring constantly, until the batter thickens into a stiff, smooth paste (15–20 minutes).
3. Pour into a greased flat tray; smooth and let cool completely (1 hour minimum).
4. Stir-fry pork with mushrooms, fish sauce, and a pinch of sugar.
5. Slice rice cake into wedges; top with pork, peanuts, fried shallots, and spring onion. Drizzle with nuoc cham.
tips: Constant stirring while cooking is essential  -  stop for even a moment and the bottom burns. Use a wooden spoon and work the whole surface.

name: Chả Rươi
description: A seasonal northern delicacy made from rươi (polychaete worms) that emerge briefly each autumn  -  mixed with egg, dill, and tangerine peel into a fragrant patty and pan-fried until crisp. An extraordinary dish that tastes of umami, herbs, and October in Hà Nội.
tagline: Fried seasonal polychaete worm patties with dill
ingredients:
- 300g fresh rươi worms (seasonal, autumn)
- 3 eggs
- A large handful of fresh dill, finely chopped
- Zest of 1 tangerine
- 1 tsp fish sauce
- ½ tsp turmeric
- Neutral oil for frying
instructions:
1. Clean worms by soaking in salted water; drain and rinse well.
2. Combine worms with beaten egg, dill, tangerine zest, fish sauce, and turmeric. Mix gently.
3. Heat oil in a non-stick pan. Pour in a thin layer of mixture (like a thick omelette).
4. Cook on medium until set on the bottom and golden; flip carefully and cook the other side.
5. Slice into wedges and serve with rice and a simple dipping sauce.
tips: Rươi is only available a few weeks a year  -  traditionally at the 9th and 11th lunar months. If you encounter them, make this immediately.

name: Gà Luộc
description: A whole chicken poached gently in salted water until just cooked through  -  the northern method prized for the resulting golden skin and tender meat. Served with a salt-pepper-lime dipping sauce and always alongside the poaching broth as soup.
tagline: Poached chicken with salt, pepper, and lime dip
ingredients:
- 1 whole chicken (1.5kg)
- 2 tsp salt
- 1 tbsp turmeric (optional, for golden skin)
- For dipping: 2 tsp salt, 1 tsp black pepper, juice of 1 lime, 1 fresh chilli
instructions:
1. Rub chicken with salt and turmeric inside and out. Rest 10 minutes.
2. Lower chicken into a pot of cold water. Bring slowly to a gentle simmer (not a rolling boil).
3. Poach on very low heat for 30–35 minutes. The water should barely tremble.
4. Remove chicken; rest 10 minutes before chopping.
5. Make dipping sauce: combine salt, pepper, lime juice, and minced chilli.
6. Chop chicken through the bone into pieces. Serve with the dip and a bowl of the poaching broth.
tips: The secret to golden skin is a cold-water start and a very gentle simmer. Vigorous boiling makes the skin shrink and the meat tough.

name: Giò Lụa
description: Vietnamese silky pork sausage  -  lean pork ground to a paste with fish sauce and wrapped tightly in banana leaves before being boiled. The result is dense, smooth, and subtly savoury. A northern staple present at every family table and every holiday spread.
tagline: Silky pork sausage in banana leaf, the northern classic
ingredients:
- 500g pork leg, very lean, chilled
- 1.5 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp baking powder
- 1 tsp sugar
- Banana leaves (softened in boiling water)
instructions:
1. Slice pork very thin; grind in a food processor until it forms a smooth, sticky paste (5–8 minutes). Keep the meat chilled throughout  -  warmth ruins the texture.
2. Add fish sauce, baking powder, and sugar; blend another 2 minutes until very smooth.
3. Lay out a double layer of banana leaf. Spoon pork paste into a cylinder; roll tightly and tie at both ends with string.
4. Boil in water for 55–60 minutes. Cool completely before slicing.
tips: Temperature control is everything  -  the pork paste must stay cold throughout processing or the proteins won't bind correctly and you'll get a grainy sausage.

name: Vịt Luộc
description: Whole duck poached northern-style  -  lightly salted and served with a pungent fermented ginger dipping sauce (mắm gừng) that cuts the richness perfectly. A dish that appears at every northern family gathering.
tagline: Poached duck with fermented ginger dipping sauce
ingredients:
- 1 whole duck (1.8–2kg)
- 2 tsp salt
- 1 knob ginger, bruised
- For mắm gừng: 3 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp grated fresh ginger, 1 tsp sugar, 1 red chilli, lime juice
instructions:
1. Rub duck inside and out with salt; stuff cavity with bruised ginger.
2. Place in a large pot; cover with cold water. Bring to a slow simmer.
3. Poach at a very gentle simmer for 45–50 minutes until thigh juices run clear.
4. Rest duck for 15 minutes before chopping.
5. Make mắm gừng: combine fish sauce, grated ginger, sugar, chilli, and a squeeze of lime. Balance to taste.
6. Chop duck through the bone; arrange on a platter. Serve dipping sauce alongside.
tips: Duck fat renders better with slow, gentle heat. A hard boil will tighten the meat  -  patience gives you silky, yielding flesh.

name: Nem Chua
description: Fermented pork rolls  -  raw pork mixed with toasted rice powder, wrapped tightly in banana leaf and left to cure for 3–5 days until tangy and slightly sour. Eaten as a snack with garlic and chilli, or alongside beer in Hà Nội's bia hơi stalls.
tagline: Fermented pork rolls, tangy and funky
ingredients:
- 300g lean pork, finely minced
- 100g pork skin (bì), boiled and julienned
- 3 tbsp toasted rice powder (thính)
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- Garlic cloves, fresh chilli
- Banana leaves or plastic wrap for wrapping
instructions:
1. Combine pork, pork skin, toasted rice powder, fish sauce, and sugar. Mix thoroughly.
2. Divide into 10 small portions. Shape each into a small log; place a slice of garlic and chilli on top.
3. Wrap tightly in banana leaf or plastic wrap, securing the ends.
4. Leave at room temperature for 3–5 days (warmer climates = less time). Taste daily  -  ready when tangy and firm.
5. Eat as is  -  no cooking needed.
tips: The toasted rice powder (thính) is crucial  -  it feeds the fermentation and gives the final roll its distinctive dry, tangy texture. Don't skip it.

name: Đậu Phụ Chiên Mắm Tôm
description: Golden fried tofu cubes served with a bowl of mắm tôm (fermented shrimp paste) beaten with lime juice, sugar, and chilli. The pungent sauce clings to the crispy tofu. A daily northern side dish that sounds simple and tastes extraordinary.
tagline: Crispy tofu with fermented shrimp paste dip
ingredients:
- 400g firm tofu, cut into cubes
- Oil for frying
- For mắm tôm sauce: 2 tbsp mắm tôm, juice of 1 lime, 1 tsp sugar, 1 red chilli minced, 2 tbsp warm water
instructions:
1. Drain tofu thoroughly; pat dry with paper towel.
2. Heat oil in a pan; fry tofu on all sides until golden and crisp (7–10 minutes). Drain.
3. Whisk mắm tôm with lime juice, sugar, and water until airy and pale. Add chilli.
4. Serve fried tofu alongside the sauce for dipping.
tips: Beating the mắm tôm vigorously with lime juice changes its texture completely  -  it becomes frothy and much more pleasant to eat.

name: Cà Muối
description: Quick-pickled eggplant  -  the northern household condiment. Slender Asian eggplants salted and left to ferment lightly until softened and pleasantly sour. Eaten alongside rice and whatever else is on the table. Makes everything better.
tagline: Lightly fermented pickled eggplant
ingredients:
- 4 small Asian eggplants
- 1 tbsp salt
- ½ tsp sugar
- 2 cloves garlic, sliced
- Fresh chilli (optional)
instructions:
1. Halve or quarter eggplants lengthwise; cut into 5cm pieces.
2. Combine salt, sugar, garlic, and eggplant in a jar or bowl. Mix thoroughly.
3. Press down firmly; cover and leave at room temperature for 24–48 hours.
4. Taste after 24 hours  -  ready when slightly soured and softened.
tips: The eggplant shrinks dramatically as it pickles. Start with more than you think you need.

name: Thịt Đông
description: Northern Vietnam's winter cold cut  -  pork belly and trotters simmered with fish sauce and a little sugar until the gelatin-rich broth sets into a firm jelly when chilled. Sliced and eaten cold with rice and pickled vegetables. Only made when the weather is cold enough for it to set naturally.
tagline: Northern pork jelly, made only in the cold months
ingredients:
- 500g pork belly, skin on
- 2 pork trotters, chopped into pieces
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp sugar
- 5 dried wood ear mushrooms, soaked
- White pepper
instructions:
1. Blanch pork belly and trotters in boiling water 5 minutes; drain and rinse.
2. Simmer pork with fish sauce, sugar, and mushrooms in just enough water to cover, for 75–80 minutes until very tender.
3. Remove bones from trotters; shred meat. Chop pork belly into pieces.
4. Pour everything (including broth) into a shallow dish. Let cool to room temperature.
5. Refrigerate until fully set, at least 4 hours. Slice and serve cold.
tips: This dish needs cold weather (or a fridge). In Hà Nội, it's made outdoors in December  -  the winter air sets it naturally overnight.

name: Nộm Hoa Chuối
description: Banana blossom salad, thinly shredded and dressed with lime juice, fish sauce, peanuts, and fresh herbs. Crisp, sour, and refreshing  -  a northern salad served before the heavier dishes arrive.
tagline: Shredded banana blossom salad with peanuts and herbs
ingredients:
- 1 banana blossom, outer leaves removed
- Juice of 2 limes (for soaking)
- 150g cooked shredded chicken or prawns
- 3 tbsp roasted peanuts, roughly crushed
- Fresh mint, perilla, coriander
- Nuoc cham: fish sauce, lime, sugar, chilli, garlic
instructions:
1. Halve banana blossom; shred very thinly into a bowl of lime water to prevent browning. Soak 15 minutes; drain and squeeze dry.
2. Make nuoc cham: combine fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, minced garlic and chilli. Balance sweet, sour, salty.
3. Toss banana blossom with chicken or prawns, herbs, and dressing.
4. Pile onto a plate; scatter peanuts over.
tips: Wear gloves when handling banana blossom  -  the sap stains skin brown. The lime water soak stops both browning and bitterness.

name: Ốc Hấp Sả
description: Steamed snails with lemongrass and chilli  -  a Hà Nội street speciality sold on tiny stools outside beer stalls from dusk onwards. Messy, fragrant, and deeply satisfying to eat with a toothpick.
tagline: Lemongrass steamed snails, a Hà Nội street staple
ingredients:
- 600g freshwater snails, cleaned
- 3 lemongrass stalks, bruised and tied in a knot
- 3 red chillies, split
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp rice wine or beer
- Fresh ginger slices
- Lime juice, salt-pepper dip to serve
instructions:
1. Clean snails by soaking in salted water for 1 hour with a little oil; drain and rinse.
2. In a wok or pot, combine snails, lemongrass, chilli, ginger, fish sauce, and rice wine.
3. Cover and steam on high heat for 15–18 minutes, tossing once halfway.
4. Serve the snails in their shells with a small side of lime juice mixed with salt and pepper for dipping.
tips: The trick to eating them is a sharp toothpick or safety pin to extract the snail in one smooth pull. Practice makes perfect.

name: Bánh Cốm
description: A Hà Nội autumn confection made from cốm  -  young green glutinous rice harvested before it fully matures, pounded flat and fragrant. Layered with sweetened mung bean and coloured vivid green, cut into squares. A seasonal, fleeting pleasure.
tagline: Green young rice cake, Hà Nội's autumn sweet
ingredients:
- 300g cốm (dried young green glutinous rice)
- 200g split mung bean, steamed and sweetened with 50g sugar
- 50g desiccated coconut
- 2 tbsp green food colouring (or pandan juice)
- 3 tbsp sugar
- Banana leaf to line tray
instructions:
1. Grind cốm coarsely. Mix with sugar and enough pandan water to colour and moisten into a sticky mass.
2. Mix mung bean with coconut and a pinch of salt.
3. Line a square tray with banana leaf. Press half the cốm mixture in evenly.
4. Spread mung bean filling over. Top with remaining cốm; press firmly.
5. Cover and leave to set 2 hours. Slice into squares.
tips: Cốm is only available in the autumn harvest season (September–November). Out of season, use green-coloured glutinous rice flour as a substitute.

name: Chè Lam
description: A chewy northern confection  -  roasted glutinous rice flour mixed with malt syrup, ginger, and sesame into a sticky candy that stretches when you pull it. Street candy from rural Hà Tây, now hard to find outside the northern countryside.
tagline: Chewy roasted rice and malt candy with ginger
ingredients:
- 200g glutinous rice flour, dry-toasted until pale golden
- 150g malt syrup or barley malt extract
- 50g sugar
- 1 tsp ground ginger
- 3 tbsp toasted sesame seeds
- Oil for hands
instructions:
1. Combine malt syrup and sugar in a saucepan; heat on low until sugar dissolves and mixture is pourable.
2. Remove from heat; quickly stir in toasted rice flour, ginger, and sesame. The mixture will be very stiff.
3. Oil your hands and a work surface. Turn out the dough and knead briefly until smooth.
4. Roll into a log; cut into small pieces.
5. Wrap individually in paper or wax paper as they're sticky.
tips: Work quickly  -  the candy firms up as it cools. If it gets too stiff to shape, warm it briefly in the oven.

name: Chè Kho
description: Northern mung bean sweet paste  -  mung beans cooked with sugar until deeply thick, fragrant with vanilla and shredded coconut. Scooped into small mounds and eaten at room temperature. A lunar new year staple from northern households.
tagline: Sweet mung bean paste, a northern Tết staple
ingredients:
- 400g split mung beans, soaked 2 hours
- 250g sugar
- 1 tsp vanilla extract
- 3 tbsp desiccated coconut
- A pinch of salt
instructions:
1. Steam mung beans until very soft, about 30 minutes. While hot, mash or blend completely smooth.
2. Combine mung bean paste with sugar, salt, and vanilla in a saucepan.
3. Cook on medium-low, stirring constantly, for 20–25 minutes until the paste is very thick and pulls away from the sides.
4. Stir in coconut. Pour into a greased dish; smooth top.
5. Cool completely; cut into squares or scoop into rounds.
tips: Stir constantly in the final stage  -  the paste scorches easily once it gets thick. A wooden spoon and a strong arm are your best tools.

name: Trà Sen
description: Lotus tea  -  green tea scented with fresh lotus stamens by packing tea leaves inside open lotus flowers and leaving them overnight to absorb the fragrance. A Hà Nội obsession, priced high for good reason. Floral, grassy, and impossibly delicate.
tagline: Green tea scented overnight inside lotus flowers
ingredients:
- 30g high-quality green tea (Thái Nguyên or similar)
- 8–10 fresh lotus flowers, ideally open at dusk
- Hot water (80°C, not boiling)
instructions:
1. At dusk when lotus flowers open, carefully open each flower further and pack tea leaves between the stamens.
2. Close the petals gently and secure loosely. Leave overnight (8 hours minimum).
3. In the morning, remove the tea from the flowers  -  it will be fragrant with lotus scent.
4. Brew at 80°C for 2–3 minutes. Do not over-steep.
tips: The flowers must be alive and open  -  cut flowers that have been in a vase for days have lost most of their fragrance. This is why trà sen is expensive.

name: Sấu Dầm
description: Indian jujube (sấu) preserved in salt and sugar, then steeped in cold water to make a tangy, pale yellow drink. A uniquely northern summer drink  -  sấu trees line the old streets of Hà Nội and their tart fruits fall in June.
tagline: Salted Indian jujube summer drink, pure Hà Nội
ingredients:
- 300g fresh sấu (Indian jujube) or green plums
- 2 tbsp salt
- 3 tbsp sugar
- 1L cold water
- Ice to serve
instructions:
1. Score each sấu around the circumference. Combine with salt and sugar in a jar; shake to coat.
2. Leave at room temperature for at least 4 hours (or overnight) until the fruit softens and releases liquid.
3. To serve: place 3–4 sấu in a glass, add cold water, stir, and top with ice.
4. Drink the liquid and eat the soft fruit  -  it's the best part.
tips: Green plums make an excellent substitute if sấu is unavailable. The drink should be sour-salty-sweet  -  adjust sugar or salt to taste.

name: Nước Sâm
description: A northern herbal cooling drink made by simmering dried herbs  -  typically corn silk, imperata root, and dried sugarcane  -  until the liquid turns amber and faintly sweet. Drunk cold in summer as a traditional body coolant.
tagline: Northern herbal cooling tea, amber and sweet
ingredients:
- 20g corn silk (dried)
- 15g imperata root (bạch mao căn, dried)
- 10g dried sugarcane pieces or 2 tbsp rock sugar
- 1.5L water
- Ice to serve
instructions:
1. Rinse dried herbs briefly. Combine with water in a pot.
2. Bring to a boil; reduce heat and simmer 35–40 minutes.
3. Add rock sugar; stir to dissolve. Adjust sweetness.
4. Strain and cool completely. Serve over ice.
tips: These herbs are available at Vietnamese herbal medicine shops. The drink is very lightly sweet  -  it shouldn't taste like a dessert, just gently cooling.

name: Rượu Nếp Than
description: Black glutinous rice wine  -  fermented black sticky rice that turns the liquid a deep ruby-purple and tastes faintly sweet, mildly alcoholic, and richly complex. A northern highland specialty, traditionally made by Mường communities.
tagline: Black sticky rice wine, deep ruby and slightly sweet
ingredients:
- 400g black glutinous rice
- 1 rice wine yeast ball (men rượu)
- Clean water as needed
instructions:
1. Steam black glutinous rice until fully cooked and sticky, about 40 minutes.
2. Spread rice on a clean tray to cool to body temperature.
3. Crumble yeast ball over the rice; mix well.
4. Pack into a clean glass jar; loosely cover (not airtight) and leave at room temperature.
5. After 2–3 days the rice will have released purple-black liquid. Strain and bottle. Drink chilled.
tips: Fermentation speed depends on temperature. In hot weather (30°C), 2 days may be enough. Cool weather needs 3–4 days. Taste daily.

name: Trà Hoa Cúc
description: Chrysanthemum flower tea  -  dried chrysanthemum flowers steeped in hot water until the liquid turns pale gold, lightly sweetened with rock sugar. A northern evening ritual, served hot in winter and cooled with ice in summer.
tagline: Chrysanthemum flower tea, pale gold and calming
ingredients:
- 15g dried chrysanthemum flowers (hoa cúc)
- 800ml boiling water
- 2 tbsp rock sugar (or to taste)
- Goji berries (optional)
instructions:
1. Rinse dried flowers briefly in cold water.
2. Pour boiling water over flowers; steep for 5–7 minutes until pale gold.
3. Strain; dissolve rock sugar into the hot tea.
4. Add goji berries if using. Serve hot, or cool and serve over ice.
tips: Don't over-steep  -  beyond 8 minutes, chrysanthemum tea turns slightly bitter. The colour should be a pale, clear gold, not dark amber.

name: Cao Lầu
description: Hội An's signature noodle dish  -  thick chewy noodles with char-siu-style pork, crisp croutons, and fresh herbs. Made with water from ancient Cham wells.
tagline: Hội An's noodle that belongs to no one else
ingredients:
- 200g cao lầu noodles (or thick rice noodles)
- 200g pork shoulder, sliced thin
- 2 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tbsp five-spice powder
- 2 tbsp hoisin sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- 100g rice crackers or croutons
- Bean sprouts, fresh herbs (mint, perilla)
- Lime, chili
instructions:
1. Marinate pork in soy sauce, five-spice, hoisin, sugar for 30 min.
2. Char pork over high heat or broil until caramelized edges, 8–10 min.
3. Cook noodles per package; drain and rinse.
4. Assemble: noodles, pork slices, bean sprouts, herbs, crackers.
5. Dress with pork drippings and a squeeze of lime.
tips: The crackers are essential  -  they absorb the pork juices and add crunch. Don't skip them.

name: Bún Chả Cá Đà Nẵng
description: Đà Nẵng's beloved fish cake noodle soup  -  golden-fried fish cakes bobbing in a clear, lightly sweet broth seasoned with turmeric.
tagline: Đà Nẵng mornings in a bowl
ingredients:
- 300g bún (rice vermicelli)
- 400g white fish (snapper or mackerel), minced
- 1 tsp turmeric powder
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 stalk lemongrass, minced
- Fish sauce, salt, pepper
- 1.5L pork or fish bone broth
- Scallions, dill, lime
instructions:
1. Mix minced fish with turmeric, garlic, lemongrass, salt, pepper; shape into oval cakes.
2. Fry cakes in oil until golden, about 3 min per side.
3. Bring broth to a simmer; season with fish sauce.
4. Serve noodles in bowls, ladle broth over, add fish cakes, top with scallions and dill.
tips: The dill is non-negotiable in Đà Nẵng  -  it's what makes the dish smell right.

name: Phở Sate Huế
description: A fiery Huế variation on phở  -  the broth is enriched with a thick sate paste of lemongrass, chili, and shrimp paste that turns it crimson and complex.
tagline: Phở with the heat of Huế
ingredients:
- 200g phở noodles
- 1.2L beef or chicken broth
- 3 tbsp sate paste (lemongrass, chili, shrimp paste)
- 150g beef, sliced thin
- Bean sprouts, Vietnamese basil
- Lime, fish sauce
instructions:
1. Stir sate paste into hot broth; simmer 5 min.
2. Season with fish sauce.
3. Blanch noodles; arrange in bowls.
4. Top with raw beef slices; ladle boiling broth over to cook the beef.
5. Serve with sprouts, basil, lime.
tips: Start with 2 tbsp sate if you're heat-sensitive  -  it's potent.

name: Bún Thịt Nướng Miền Trung
description: Rice vermicelli with Central-style grilled pork  -  the marinade leans heavily on lemongrass and turmeric, giving the meat a yellow tint and floral heat.
tagline: Fragrant grilled pork over cool noodles
ingredients:
- 200g bún
- 300g pork shoulder, sliced thin
- 2 stalks lemongrass, minced
- 1 tsp turmeric
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp sugar
- Lettuce, cucumber, mint, bean sprouts
- Nước chấm dipping sauce
