instructions:
1. Marinate pork with lemongrass, turmeric, fish sauce, sugar for 1 hr.
2. Grill over charcoal or high-heat pan until caramelized, 2–3 min per side.
3. Arrange noodles in bowls with lettuce, cucumber, herbs, sprouts.
4. Top with pork; drizzle with nước chấm.
tips: A little charcoal smokiness is irreplaceable  -  grill outdoors if you can.

name: Bánh Canh Huế
description: Huế's thick tapioca noodle soup with pork and shrimp in a richer, more savory broth than the southern version, often finished with a spoonful of shrimp paste.
tagline: Chewy and bold from Huế
ingredients:
- 300g bánh canh noodles (tapioca)
- 300g pork ribs
- 150g shrimp, peeled
- 1 tbsp mắm ruốc (shrimp paste)
- Fish sauce, salt, pepper
- Scallions, crispy shallots
instructions:
1. Simmer ribs in 1.5L water for 30 min; skim.
2. Add shrimp and noodles; cook 5 min.
3. Stir in shrimp paste; season with fish sauce, salt.
4. Ladle into bowls; top with scallions and crispy shallots.
tips: Mắm ruốc gives the broth its depth  -  add in small amounts to taste, it's very pungent.

name: Bún Số 8 Quy Nhơn
description: Quy Nhơn's peanut-forward noodle dish  -  thin flat noodles tossed in a savory-sweet sauce with fried tofu, peanuts, and bean sprouts.
tagline: Quy Nhơn's nutty toss noodles
ingredients:
- 200g flat rice noodles (bún số 8)
- 100g firm tofu, cubed and fried
- 3 tbsp roasted peanuts, crushed
- 2 tbsp hoisin sauce
- 1 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tsp sesame oil
- Bean sprouts, scallions, chili
instructions:
1. Cook noodles; drain and rinse with cold water.
2. Mix hoisin, soy sauce, sesame oil.
3. Toss noodles with sauce, tofu, bean sprouts.
4. Top with peanuts, scallions, chili.
tips: Serve at room temperature  -  this is a dry noodle dish, not a soup.

name: Mì Quảng Gà
description: A chicken version of the Quảng Nam classic  -  turmeric-yellow noodles with braised chicken thighs, peanuts, sesame crackers, and just a splash of broth.
tagline: Golden noodles, barely broth
ingredients:
- 300g mì quảng noodles (or wide flat rice noodles)
- 2 chicken thighs, bone-in
- 1 tsp turmeric
- 1 onion, sliced
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- Sesame rice crackers, roasted peanuts
- Banana flower, lettuce, mint, bean sprouts
- Lime, chili
instructions:
1. Brown chicken with onion and turmeric; add 400ml water, fish sauce; braise 25 min.
2. Remove chicken, shred meat, reserve broth.
3. Blanch noodles; arrange in bowls with vegetables and herbs.
4. Add chicken; ladle a small amount of broth.
5. Top with peanuts, crackers, lime, chili.
tips: Use just a little broth  -  mì quảng is a 'wet noodle' dish, not a soup.

name: Bún Sứa Nha Trang
description: Nha Trang's unique jellyfish noodle soup  -  chewy translucent jellyfish strips in a light seafood broth, topped with crispy pork rinds.
tagline: The sea in every strange, chewy bite
ingredients:
- 200g bún
- 150g prepared jellyfish strips
- 1L seafood or pork bone broth
- 50g pork rinds (chicharrón)
- Fish sauce, salt
- Scallions, herb plate (perilla, basil)
instructions:
1. Prepare jellyfish: soak in cold water 1 hr to remove salt; blanch briefly.
2. Season broth with fish sauce, salt; bring to simmer.
3. Arrange noodles in bowl; add jellyfish strips.
4. Ladle broth; top with pork rinds and scallions.
5. Serve with fresh herbs.
tips: Jellyfish should be translucent and springy  -  if it's rubbery, it was blanched too long.

name: Bún Bò Chả Cua Huế
description: The Huế royal variant of bún bò  -  augmented with crab paste dumplings (chả cua) that add a briny sweetness to the already complex lemongrass broth.
tagline: Bún bò's most regal form
ingredients:
- 400g bún (thick rice noodles)
- 500g beef shank, sliced
- 200g pork hocks
- 2 tbsp mắm ruốc (shrimp paste)
- 3 stalks lemongrass, bruised
- 2 tbsp chili oil
- 150g crab meat + 1 egg (for chả cua dumplings)
- Banana blossoms, bean sprouts, lime
instructions:
1. Simmer beef shank and pork hocks with lemongrass 40 min; skim.
2. Mix crab meat with egg, season; steam in small ramekins or wrap in banana leaf until set.
3. Dissolve shrimp paste in broth; add chili oil.
4. Assemble: noodles, beef, pork, crab cake; ladle broth; top with banana blossom.
tips: The chả cua should be added to the broth just before serving to stay silky, not rubbery.

name: Hủ Tiếu Đà Nẵng
description: A Central-inflected hủ tiếu  -  cleaner broth than the southern version, lighter and lemongrass-tinged, served with thin pork slices and seafood.
tagline: The cleaner cousin of southern hủ tiếu
ingredients:
- 200g hủ tiếu noodles
- 1.2L pork bone broth
- 1 stalk lemongrass, bruised
- 100g pork loin, sliced thin
- 100g shrimp, peeled
- Fish sauce, salt
- Scallions, bean sprouts, lime
instructions:
1. Simmer broth with lemongrass 15 min; remove lemongrass.
2. Season with fish sauce, salt.
3. Cook noodles; divide into bowls.
4. Blanch pork and shrimp in broth; arrange on noodles.
5. Ladle broth; top with scallions and sprouts.
tips: The lemongrass infusion is what distinguishes this from southern hủ tiếu  -  don't skip it.

name: Cơm Gà Hội An
description: Hội An's beloved chicken rice  -  poached chicken over turmeric-yellow rice cooked in chicken fat and broth, served with ginger fish sauce dip.
tagline: Hội An's golden chicken rice
ingredients:
- 2 cups jasmine rice
- 1 whole chicken (1.5kg)
- 1 tsp turmeric
- 2 shallots, halved
- 3 cups chicken broth (from poaching)
- 2 tbsp chicken fat
- Fresh ginger, fish sauce, lime, chili (for dipping sauce)
- Cilantro, cucumber
instructions:
1. Poach chicken with shallots and ginger in salted water 30 min; reserve broth.
2. Toast rice in chicken fat with turmeric 2 min; add 2.5 cups broth.
3. Cook rice until done, 18 min.
4. Slice chicken; serve over rice with cucumber and cilantro.
5. Make dipping sauce: ginger, fish sauce, lime juice, chili.
tips: Cooling the chicken in its broth keeps it juicy. Slice only just before serving.

name: Cơm Âm Phủ Huế
description: Huế's 'underworld rice'  -  an elaborate platter of rice served with a precise arrangement of over a dozen small accompaniments: grilled meats, fried eggs, pickles, pork skin.
tagline: A Huế feast on one plate
ingredients:
- 2 cups cooked jasmine rice
- 2 eggs, fried
- 100g pork belly, grilled and sliced
- 50g pork skin, boiled and sliced
- 50g grilled shrimp
- Pickled carrots and daikon
- Grilled banana pepper
- Sesame, scallion oil
- Fish sauce dipping sauce
instructions:
1. Cook rice.
2. Prepare each component separately: grill pork belly, fry eggs, grill shrimp.
3. Boil pork skin until tender; slice thin.
4. Arrange rice in center of plate; surround with each component in sections.
5. Drizzle with scallion oil; serve dipping sauce on side.
tips: The artful arrangement is the point  -  take time to plate it beautifully.

name: Xôi Huế
description: Huế-style sticky rice served with savory toppings: dried shrimp, mung bean paste, fried shallots, and sometimes pandan-scented with a dusting of sesame.
tagline: Sticky rice the Huế way  -  layered and savory
ingredients:
- 2 cups glutinous rice, soaked overnight
- 50g dried shrimp
- 100g split mung beans, steamed and mashed
- 3 shallots, thinly sliced and fried
- 1 tbsp sesame seeds
- Salt, sugar, fish sauce
instructions:
1. Steam glutinous rice 30 min until tender.
2. Reconstitute dried shrimp; sauté with fish sauce and sugar.
3. Press mung bean paste into a layer.
4. Serve rice topped with mung bean layer, shrimp, shallots, sesame.
5. Season with fish sauce to taste.
tips: Layer the toppings in order  -  mung bean first, then shrimp, then shallots  -  for the right texture in each bite.

name: Cháo Cá Huế
description: Huế-style rice porridge with river fish  -  seasoned with galangal and lemongrass, ladled over the fish rather than cooked with it to keep the flesh tender.
tagline: Silky porridge with the river's best
ingredients:
- 1 cup jasmine rice
- 400g white river fish (or tilapia), filleted
- 2 slices galangal
- 1 stalk lemongrass, bruised
- Fish sauce, salt, white pepper
- Scallions, ginger, fresh chili
instructions:
1. Simmer rice with 1.5L water, galangal, lemongrass until porridge consistency, 35 min.
2. Remove aromatics; season with fish sauce, salt.
3. Poach fish gently in the congee for 4 min until just cooked.
4. Serve with scallions, ginger strips, white pepper, chili.
tips: Don't stir fish into the congee  -  lay it on top to keep the flesh intact.

name: Cơm Niêu Đất
description: Clay pot rice cooked over charcoal  -  the crust on the bottom of the pot is prized. Served simply with braised pork and a sharp ginger dipping sauce.
tagline: The scorched bottom is the prize
ingredients:
- 1.5 cups jasmine rice
- 200g braised pork belly, sliced
- 2 cups water
- Salt
- Ginger dipping sauce: fresh ginger, fish sauce, lime, chili
- Scallion oil
instructions:
1. Rinse rice; place in clay pot with water and salt.
2. Cook over medium heat until water absorbs, then reduce to lowest heat.
3. Continue 10–12 min to form crust on bottom.
4. Remove from heat; let rest 5 min.
5. Serve with pork belly, scallion oil, and ginger sauce for dipping the crust.
tips: Listen  -  when the sizzling sound changes to a dry crackle, the crust is forming. Don't lift the lid too early.

name: Cơm Tấm Bì Huế
description: Huế's take on broken rice  -  served with shredded pork skin (bì) tossed in toasted rice powder and herbs, alongside grilled pork and a fried egg.
tagline: Broken rice dressed in the Huế way
ingredients:
- 2 cups cooked broken rice (cơm tấm)
- 150g pork skin, boiled and julienned
- 3 tbsp toasted rice powder
- Fresh herbs (mint, perilla)
- 2 eggs, fried
- 150g pork chop, marinated and grilled
- Nước chấm dipping sauce
- Pickled carrots and daikon
instructions:
1. Toss pork skin with rice powder, herbs, salt.
2. Grill pork chop over high heat, 3 min per side.
3. Fry eggs.
4. Serve rice topped with bì, pork chop, fried egg.
5. Add pickles; serve nước chấm on side.
tips: Toast rice powder in a dry pan until golden  -  it adds a nutty fragrance that defines this dish.

name: Bánh Nậm
description: A flat, steamed Huế dumpling  -  thin rice flour skin pressed flat, filled with shrimp and pork, then wrapped in banana leaf into a neat rectangular parcel.
tagline: Paper-thin Huế elegance, wrapped in leaf
ingredients:
- 200g rice flour
- 2 tbsp tapioca starch
- 400ml water
- 150g shrimp, minced
- 100g ground pork
- 2 shallots, minced
- Fish sauce, pepper
- Banana leaves for wrapping
- Scallion oil for serving
instructions:
1. Mix rice flour and tapioca with water; cook over low heat until thickened.
2. Sauté shallots, pork, shrimp with fish sauce and pepper.
3. Lay banana leaf flat; spread thin layer of dough, add filling, fold into flat rectangle.
4. Steam 15 min.
5. Serve with scallion oil and fish sauce.
tips: The dough layer must be thin  -  you should almost see the filling through it.

name: Bánh Ướt Cuốn Thịt
description: Fresh steamed rice sheets rolled with grilled pork and herbs  -  the Central version is wider and thinner than the Northern bánh cuốn, eaten dipped rather than souped.
tagline: Silky fresh sheets rolled to order
ingredients:
- 150g rice flour + 2 tbsp tapioca starch + 300ml water (batter)
- 200g grilled pork, sliced thin
- Fresh herbs: mint, perilla, lettuce
- Cucumber strips
- Nước chấm
instructions:
1. Mix batter; rest 20 min.
2. Pour thin layer onto oiled steamer plate; steam 2 min until translucent.
3. Remove carefully with spatula.
4. Place pork, herbs, cucumber on rice sheet; roll up.
5. Serve immediately with nước chấm.
tips: Work quickly  -  fresh rice sheets dry out and tear if left uncovered for more than a minute.

name: Bánh Tét Miền Trung
description: The Central version of the Tết rice cake  -  smaller and more cylindrical than the southern version, with a saltier, savorier mung bean and pork filling.
tagline: The festive log of Tết
ingredients:
- 2 cups glutinous rice, soaked overnight
- 200g split mung beans, soaked
- 200g pork belly, cut into strips
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- Salt, pepper
- Banana leaves for wrapping
- Kitchen twine
instructions:
1. Season mung beans with salt; steam until soft, mash lightly.
2. Season pork with fish sauce, pepper.
3. Lay banana leaves; add rice layer, mung bean, pork, another rice layer.
4. Roll tightly into cylinder; tie with twine.
5. Boil 3–4 hours; cool before slicing.
tips: Keep adding water during boiling to keep the cakes submerged  -  drying out ruins the texture.

name: Ram Ít Huế
description: A paired dumpling unique to Huế  -  one soft steamed bánh ít (glutinous rice with shrimp filling) paired with one crispy deep-fried version. Served together.
tagline: One soft, one crispy  -  always served in pairs
ingredients:
- 200g glutinous rice flour
- 100ml hot water
- 150g shrimp, minced
- 50g ground pork
- 2 shallots, minced
- Fish sauce, pepper
- Oil for frying
- Banana leaves
instructions:
1. Mix glutinous flour and hot water; knead until smooth dough.
2. Cook filling: sauté shrimp, pork, shallots with fish sauce.
3. Divide dough; wrap filling; shape into small domes.
4. Steam half on banana leaf squares, 12 min.
5. Deep-fry remaining half until golden, 3–4 min.
6. Serve one steamed and one fried per portion.
tips: The contrast between textures is the whole point  -  eat one of each in the same bite.

name: Bánh Mì Hội An
description: Hội An's legendary bánh mì  -  a shorter, crispier baguette stuffed with Vietnamese pâté, char-siu pork, pickles, jalapeño, and a drizzle of housemade sauce.
tagline: The bánh mì that made Hội An famous
ingredients:
- 1 short crispy baguette (25cm)
- 2 tbsp chicken liver pâté
- 100g char-siu or lemongrass pork, sliced
- Pickled daikon and carrot
- Fresh jalapeño, sliced
- Cucumber strips
- Cilantro
- 1 tbsp housemade sauce (soy + hoisin + chili)
instructions:
1. Warm baguette in oven or toaster 2–3 min until crisp.
2. Split; spread pâté on one side, sauce on other.
3. Layer pork, pickles, cucumber, jalapeño, cilantro.
4. Press lightly and eat immediately.
tips: The baguette must be crispy  -  a soft roll ruins the textural contrast that makes Hội An bánh mì special.

name: Bánh In Huế
description: Huế's pressed ceremonial cake  -  made from mung bean flour packed into ornate wooden molds, these bite-sized sweets are offered at altars and served with tea.
tagline: Molded mung bean art for altar and table
ingredients:
- 200g split mung beans, soaked and steamed
- 100g sugar
- 2 tbsp pandan juice (or 1 tsp vanilla extract)
- 1 tbsp vegetable oil
- Wooden or silicone molds
instructions:
1. Steam mung beans until fully soft; press through sieve while hot.
2. Mix with sugar, pandan juice, oil while warm.
3. Cook paste over low heat, stirring constantly, until very stiff, 15 min.
4. Press tightly into oiled molds; level with spatula.
5. Unmold while still warm; cool before serving.
tips: Work quickly while the paste is warm  -  it hardens as it cools and won't press into molds evenly.

name: Bánh Tráng Đập Đà Nẵng
description: Đà Nẵng's 'smashed rice paper' snack  -  a wet rice paper sheet pressed against a crispy fried one, then drizzled with scallion oil and anchovy sauce.
tagline: Two textures, smashed together
ingredients:
- 4 sheets dried rice paper
- 2 sheets fresh wet rice paper
- 3 tbsp scallion oil (oil + minced scallions)
- 2 tbsp mắm nêm (anchovy fish sauce)
- Chili, garlic
instructions:
1. Fry or grill dried rice paper until puffed and crisp.
2. Place wet rice paper on top of crispy one.
3. Press firmly to adhere ('đập' = smash).
4. Drizzle with scallion oil.
5. Serve with mắm nêm spiked with chili and garlic for dipping.
tips: Eat immediately  -  the texture is best right when the wet paper softens the crisp one just slightly.

name: Bánh Căn Nha Trang
description: Nha Trang's beloved mini rice cakes cooked in cast-iron pans  -  each round cake crisped at the bottom with quail egg, shrimp, or squid, then flipped and dipped in mắm nêm.
tagline: Cast-iron cast-offs from the coast
ingredients:
- 200g rice flour + 50g tapioca starch + 300ml water + pinch of turmeric (batter)
- 12 quail eggs
- 100g small shrimp, peeled
- Oil for the pans
- Mắm nêm (anchovy sauce) with pineapple, garlic, chili for dipping
- Bean sprouts, shredded green mango
instructions:
1. Mix batter with turmeric and let rest 15 min.
2. Heat cast-iron mini pan wells; oil each cavity.
3. Fill halfway with batter; crack one quail egg in each; add shrimp.
4. Cover and cook 3–4 min until set and crispy on bottom.
5. Flip and cook 1 min more.
6. Serve immediately with mắm nêm and raw vegetables.
tips: The high heat is what gives the cakes their characteristic crispy bottom  -  use the highest flame you have.

name: Bê Thui Cầu Mống
description: Central Vietnam's famous char-grilled veal  -  meat from young calves, fire-charred and sliced thin, eaten wrapped in rice paper with figs and mắm nêm.
tagline: Char-grilled veal, wrapped and dipped
ingredients:
- 1kg veal leg or shoulder
- Salt, lemongrass, galangal (for rub)
- Rice paper, fresh figs or starfruit
- Pineapple, fresh herbs (basil, perilla, mint)
- Mắm nêm (anchovy sauce) with garlic and chili
instructions:
1. Rub veal with salt, minced lemongrass, galangal.
2. Roast over charcoal or in oven at 200°C, basting, until cooked through, ~90 min.
3. Slice thin against the grain.
4. Soften rice paper; fill with veal slices, fig, herbs; roll up.
5. Dip in mắm nêm.
tips: Bê thui is best when the meat is just barely pink  -  overcooked it loses its tenderness.

name: Tôm Chua Huế
description: Huế's legendary fermented shrimp pickle  -  fresh shrimp fermented with galangal, chili, and garlic until sour and pungent, served with thinly sliced pork.
tagline: Huế's pungent, prized ferment
ingredients:
- 500g fresh shrimp, peeled and deveined
- 3 tbsp cooked rice
- 4 cloves garlic, sliced
- 3cm galangal, julienned
- 2 tbsp chili flakes
- 2 tbsp sugar
- 1 tbsp salt
- Banana leaves
- Sliced pork belly, to serve
instructions:
1. Combine shrimp with cooked rice, garlic, galangal, chili, sugar, salt.
2. Mix well; pack tightly into jar.
3. Line lid with banana leaf; seal and ferment at room temperature 2–3 days.
4. When sour and pinkish-red, refrigerate.
5. Serve alongside boiled pork belly slices with rice.
tips: Taste daily  -  2 days is mild, 3 days is full Huế sourness. Don't go past 3 days at room temperature.

name: Chả Tôm Đà Nẵng
description: Grilled shrimp paste on sugarcane  -  seasoned shrimp paste shaped around a sugarcane skewer, grilled until caramelized, served with rice paper, herbs, and mắm nêm.
tagline: Sweet sugarcane meets smoky shrimp
ingredients:
- 400g shrimp, minced to paste
- 2 shallots, minced
- 1 tsp turmeric
- 1 tsp sugar
- Fish sauce, pepper
- Sugarcane stalks (or lemongrass), cut into 12cm lengths
- Rice paper, fresh herbs for wrapping
- Mắm nêm for dipping
instructions:
1. Mix shrimp paste with shallots, turmeric, sugar, fish sauce, pepper.
2. Wet hands; mold shrimp paste around sugarcane stalks.
3. Grill over charcoal 4–5 min, turning, until golden and cooked through.
4. Serve with rice paper, herbs, and mắm nêm dipping sauce.
tips: Sugarcane is not just decoration  -  chew it for the sweet juice after the shrimp is eaten.

name: Hến Xào Xả Ớt
description: Stir-fried baby clams with lemongrass and chili  -  a beloved Huế street snack, the tiny clams picked open with chopsticks over a few cold beers.
tagline: Tiny clams, big flavors, patient fingers
ingredients:
- 500g baby clams (or small clams)
- 3 stalks lemongrass, thinly sliced
- 4 fresh chilies, sliced
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- Cooking oil
- Fresh herbs, lime
instructions:
1. Purge clams in salted water 30 min; drain.
2. Heat oil; fry lemongrass, garlic, chili 1 min.
3. Add clams; stir-fry over high heat until shells open, 3–4 min.
4. Season with fish sauce, sugar.
5. Serve with fresh herbs and lime.
tips: Discard any clams that don't open  -  they're not safe to eat.

name: Gỏi Ngó Sen Tôm Thịt
description: Lotus stem salad with shrimp and pork  -  the Central version uses a more herbaceous dressing and often adds more chili than the southern counterpart.
tagline: Crunchy lotus stems in a bright, punchy dressing
ingredients:
- 200g lotus stems, sliced diagonally
- 150g shrimp, boiled and peeled
- 100g boiled pork, sliced thin
- 2 tbsp lime juice
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp sugar
- 2 fresh chilies, sliced
- 1 clove garlic, minced
- Roasted peanuts, fried shallots, fresh mint
instructions:
1. Soak lotus stems in salted vinegar water to prevent browning.
2. Mix dressing: lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, chili, garlic.
3. Drain lotus stems; toss with shrimp, pork, dressing.
4. Rest 10 min.
5. Top with peanuts, shallots, mint; serve immediately.
tips: The acid from lime keeps the lotus stems crisp  -  dress and serve right before eating.

name: Mắm Tôm Chua Ăn Kèm
description: Huế-style condiment platter for sharing  -  fermented shrimp sauce (mắm tôm chua) served with boiled pork slices, cucumber, fresh herbs, and sesame crackers for wrapping.
tagline: The Huế communal platter
ingredients:
- 200g pork belly, boiled until tender
- 4 tbsp mắm tôm chua (fermented shrimp pickle)
- Cucumber, sliced
- Fresh herbs: perilla, mint, basil
- Sesame rice crackers or rice paper
- Chili, garlic, lime
instructions:
1. Boil pork belly in salted water until tender, 15 min; cool and slice thin.
2. Mix mắm tôm chua with chili, garlic, and a squeeze of lime.
3. Arrange pork, cucumber, herbs on a large plate.
4. Put mắm tôm chua in center.
5. Wrap and dip at the table.
tips: If mắm tôm chua is new to you, start with a tiny amount  -  it's an acquired taste with intense fermented depth.

name: Chả Mực Nha Trang
description: Nha Trang's prized squid cake  -  fresh squid pounded into a springy paste, shaped and fried until golden, served plain or with a dipping sauce.
tagline: Springy squid cakes from the coast
ingredients:
- 500g fresh squid, cleaned
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 shallot, minced
- 1 tsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- Pepper
- Oil for frying
- Sweet chili sauce for serving
instructions:
1. Pound squid in mortar or blend briefly  -  should be coarsely textured, not smooth paste.
2. Mix in garlic, shallot, fish sauce, sugar, pepper.
3. Shape into patties or oval cakes.
4. Shallow fry in oil until golden, 3 min per side.
5. Serve immediately with sweet chili sauce.
tips: Don't over-blend the squid  -  chunks give the cake its characteristic bouncy bite.

name: Bánh Tráng Thịt Heo Đà Nẵng
description: Đà Nẵng's beloved DIY wrap platter  -  sliced boiled pork belly, grilled shrimp, and a mountain of herbs and starfruit on a platter with rice paper, all dipped in mắm nêm.
tagline: Đà Nẵng's hands-on communal feast
ingredients:
- 300g pork belly, boiled and sliced thin
- 200g shrimp, grilled
- Rice paper sheets
- Fresh herbs (mint, perilla, basil)
- Starfruit or green banana, sliced
- Cucumber strips
- Mắm nêm with pineapple puree, garlic, chili
instructions:
1. Boil pork belly until tender, 20 min; cool and slice.
2. Grill shrimp until pink.
3. Arrange pork, shrimp, herbs, starfruit, cucumber on a large platter.
4. Make mắm nêm: blend a little pineapple with mắm nêm, chili, garlic.
5. Soften rice paper; fill, roll, dip.
tips: The starfruit adds a tartness that cuts the pork richness  -  don't substitute it with something sweet.

name: Chè Hột Sen Nhãn
description: Huế's lotus seed and longan sweet soup  -  lotus seeds simmered until creamy, sweetened lightly, then served with dried longan for a floral, silky dessert.
tagline: Lotus and longan, serenely sweet
ingredients:
- 200g dried lotus seeds, soaked overnight
- 50g dried longan
- 80g sugar (rock sugar preferred)
- 1.5L water
- 1 tsp pandan extract (optional)
instructions:
1. Drain soaked lotus seeds; remove green inner stem if bitter.
2. Simmer in water with longan until lotus seeds are creamy, 40 min.
3. Add rock sugar; stir to dissolve.
4. Add pandan extract if using.
5. Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Remove the tiny green germ inside each lotus seed  -  it's intensely bitter and ruins the whole pot.

name: Mè Xửng Huế
description: Huế's iconic sesame candy  -  porous, airy sesame brittle made with pulled sugar, uniquely crispy-chewy, packaged in elaborate boxes as a signature Huế souvenir.
tagline: Huế's honeycomb sesame candy
ingredients:
- 300g sesame seeds, toasted
- 200g sugar
- 50g malt syrup (or glucose)
- 80ml water
- 1 tsp vinegar
instructions:
1. Cook sugar, malt syrup, water to hard-crack stage (150°C).
2. Add vinegar (creates air bubbles); fold in toasted sesame off heat.
3. Pour onto oiled surface; pull and stretch with oiled hands until airy and porous.
4. Shape into logs; slice before fully hardened.
5. Wrap in rice paper; store airtight.
tips: The pulling step is what creates the signature porous texture  -  work quickly in the brief window while it's still pliable.

name: Chè Đậu Ván Huế
description: Huế-style purple yam and white bean sweet soup  -  delicately colored and gently sweet, thickened with tapioca pearls and finished with coconut cream.
tagline: Purple and gentle, the Huế way
ingredients:
- 150g white beans (flat white or navy), soaked overnight
- 100g purple yam, cubed
- 80g sugar
- 3 tbsp small tapioca pearls
- 200ml coconut cream
- 1L water
instructions:
1. Cook beans in water until soft, 25 min.
2. Add purple yam; cook 10 more min.
3. Add sugar and tapioca pearls; stir until tapioca is translucent.
4. Drizzle coconut cream over each bowl when serving.
5. Serve warm or chilled.
tips: Don't stir too much after adding the yam  -  it will dissolve and the color will bleed unevenly.

name: Bánh Ít Gai Bình Định
description: Bình Định's black rice cake  -  glutinous rice colored black with ramie leaves (lá gai), filled with sweetened mung bean and coconut, wrapped in banana leaf.
tagline: Dark, earthy, and quietly sweet
ingredients:
- 300g glutinous rice flour
- 50g dried lá gai (ramie leaves), ground to powder, or black sesame for color
- 200g mung bean paste (cooked, sweetened)
- 50g grated coconut
- Banana leaves for wrapping
instructions:
1. Mix flour with lá gai powder; add hot water; knead to smooth dough.
2. Mix mung bean paste with grated coconut.
3. Wrap filling in dough; shape into rounded triangles.
4. Wrap in banana leaf squares.
5. Steam 25 min.
tips: Lá gai is hard to find outside Central Vietnam  -  black sesame powder is an acceptable substitute for color, though the flavor differs.

name: Kẹo Gừng Hội An
description: Hội An's ginger candy  -  chewy coconut-ginger toffee rolled in sesame, warming with real ginger heat, sold in every shop along the ancient town's lantern-lit streets.
tagline: Ginger fire wrapped in coconut chew
ingredients:
- 200g sugar
- 100ml coconut milk
- 50g fresh ginger, finely grated
- 50g roasted sesame seeds
- 1 tbsp coconut oil
instructions:
1. Cook sugar and coconut milk over medium heat, stirring, until golden and thickened.
2. Stir in ginger and coconut oil.
3. Cook to soft-ball stage (115°C).
4. Pour onto sesame-covered parchment; press flat.
5. Cut into pieces while warm; wrap in parchment.
tips: Fresh ginger is essential  -  the candy should have real heat, not just ginger flavor.

name: Nước Sâm Huế
description: Huế's cooling herbal drink  -  a complex brew of over ten dried herbs including coix seeds, lotus seeds, and pandan, sweetened and served over ice for hot afternoons.
tagline: Huế's ancient herbal coolant
ingredients:
- 20g each: coix seeds, lotus seeds
- 10g each: dried longan, dried lychee, chrysanthemum buds
- 5g licorice root
- 2 pandan leaves
- 2L water
- 100g rock sugar
instructions:
1. Rinse all dried ingredients.
2. Simmer everything with water for 35 min.
3. Add rock sugar; stir to dissolve.
4. Strain; cool to room temperature.
5. Serve over ice.
tips: The more types of herbs you use, the more complex and restorative the drink. Don't substitute aggressively.

name: Cà Phê Muối Đà Nẵng
description: Đà Nẵng's salted coffee  -  a layer of salted whipped cream floats on strong Vietnamese drip coffee, the sweet-salty-bitter contrast cutting through the heat.
tagline: Salt lifts what bitter began
ingredients:
- 2 tbsp Vietnamese ground coffee (Robusta)
- 150ml hot water
- 3 tbsp heavy cream
- 1/4 tsp sea salt
- 1 tsp sugar
- Ice
instructions:
1. Brew coffee through Vietnamese phin filter.
2. Whip cream with salt and sugar until soft peaks.
3. Fill glass with ice and black coffee.
4. Spoon salted cream gently on top  -  don't stir.
5. Drink by sipping through the cream layer.
tips: Don't stir  -  the point is to drink the bitter coffee through the salty cream, merging them on your tongue.

name: Nước Ép Mít Hội An
description: Hội An's fresh jackfruit juice  -  blended jackfruit flesh with a touch of coconut milk and crushed ice, sweetly tropical and thick enough to eat with a spoon.
tagline: Thick, tropical, impossibly sweet
ingredients:
- 300g ripe jackfruit flesh, seeds removed
- 100ml coconut milk
- 1 tbsp condensed milk (optional)
- 1 cup crushed ice
- Pinch of salt
instructions:
1. Blend jackfruit flesh with coconut milk until smooth.
2. Add condensed milk if using and a pinch of salt.
3. Blend with crushed ice until thick and slushy.
4. Pour into tall glasses; serve immediately.
tips: Ripe jackfruit should be fragrant and bright yellow  -  under-ripe is starchy and bland.

name: Chè Sen Thanh Trà
description: Huế's lotus seed and pomelo sweet soup  -  tender lotus seeds in a light syrup brightened with fresh pomelo segments and pandan fragrance.
tagline: Lotus meets citrus in the Huế way
ingredients:
- 200g dried lotus seeds, soaked overnight
- 100g pomelo flesh, broken into small pieces
- 70g sugar
- 1.5L water
- 2 pandan leaves, knotted
instructions:
1. Simmer lotus seeds with pandan in water until creamy, 35 min.
2. Add sugar; stir until dissolved.
3. Remove pandan; cool slightly.
4. Fold in pomelo pieces just before serving.
5. Serve warm or chilled.
tips: Add pomelo only at the end  -  it turns bitter if cooked.

name: Sâm Bổ Lượng Huế
description: Huế's restorative herbal cold drink  -  a jar of colorful sweet toppings (lotus seeds, longans, barley, grass jelly) in a lightly sweetened herbal liquid, served over ice.
tagline: Huế's rainbow herbal jar
ingredients:
- 50g cooked lotus seeds
- 30g dried longan, soaked
- 50g cooked pearl barley
- 50g grass jelly (thạch đen), cubed
- 50g seaweed jelly or aloe vera cubes
- 800ml light herbal syrup (chrysanthemum + rock sugar)
- Crushed ice
instructions:
1. Brew chrysanthemum with rock sugar to make the herbal syrup; cool.
2. Cook barley until soft; cool.
3. Layer lotus seeds, longan, barley, grass jelly, seaweed in jars or glasses.
4. Pour cold herbal syrup over.
5. Top with crushed ice; serve immediately.
tips: The beauty is in the layers  -  clear glass vessels show off the contrasting textures and colors.
